proposals.title: Parental Food Neophobia and Adolescent Dietary Quality: Testing a Dual-Process Intervention to Reduce Intergenerational Transmission
proposals.motivation: Food neophobia, the reluctance to try novel foods, is a significant barrier to dietary variety and quality, particularly in adolescents. Pre-2026 research by Grunert and others highlights parents as key influencers of children's food preferences, yet effective, theory-driven interventions to disrupt the intergenerational transmission of neophobia are lacking. This gap is critical as poor adolescent diets have long-term implications for health and life satisfaction.
proposals.hypothesis: A combined educational (targeting parental feeding practices and food-related life information) and exposure-based (structured tasting sessions) intervention for dual-earner parents will significantly reduce their food neophobia, which in turn will improve the dietary quality and food-related satisfaction of their adolescent children.
proposals.method: The study will employ a randomized controlled trial with pre-, post-, and follow-up measurements. Dual-earner parents with high neophobia scores (measured via the Food Neophobia Scale) will be recruited. The intervention group will receive a multi-session program: 1) an educational module based on the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ) framework to reshape feeding practices, and 2) guided exposure sessions using the Systematic Wilful Familiarization with Overt Liking (SWFOL) method for novel foods. The control group will receive generic nutrition information. Data will be analyzed using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test for measurement invariance of the CFPQ and to model the direct and spillover effects of the intervention on parental neophobia and adolescent dietary outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated, theory-based intervention model for reducing food neophobia in a high-impact family context. It will extend Grunert's work on parental drivers and measurement invariance by applying it to behavioral change, and will empirically test a spillover effect from parent to child dietary quality, contributing to both public health nutrition and consumer behavior literature.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies correlates of neophobia, this proposal uniquely integrates a validated parental feeding practices framework (CFPQ) with a structured exposure technique (SWFOL) in a dual-process intervention. It explicitly tests the causal pathway of reducing parental neophobia to improve adolescent diet, a direct application of spillover effect theory within the family food environment.

proposals.title: Bridging Behavior and Biology: A Study of Genetic and Physiological Markers in Food Neophobia Clusters Among Parents and Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Food neophobia, the reluctance to try novel foods, is a significant barrier to healthy eating, particularly in children and adolescents, with parental feeding practices playing a key role. While consumer research, including Grunert's work, has identified behavioral and attitudinal clusters of neophobia, the underlying biological predispositions remain poorly understood. Investigating these biological mediators is crucial for moving beyond descriptive clustering to explain the etiology of neophobia, which can inform more personalized and effective nutritional interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, previously identified clusters of food neophobia (e.g., high-anxiety neophobes, low-involvement neophobes) are associated with specific genetic polymorphisms related to taste perception (e.g., TAS2R38 for bitterness) and stress response (e.g., COMT), as well as differential physiological reactivity (salivary cortisol) to novel food stimuli.
proposals.method: The study will recruit a sample of dual-earner parent-adolescent dyads, leveraging expertise in family dynamics and measurement invariance. Following cluster analysis (e.g., latent profile analysis) on survey data (Food Neophobia Scale, Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire), we will genotype participants for selected candidate genes using PCR-based methods. A controlled laboratory session will measure physiological stress (salivary cortisol) and autonomic responses (heart rate variability) before and after exposure to novel food items. Data analysis will employ multivariate analysis of covariance (MANCOVA) to test for biomarker differences between clusters, controlling for demographic factors.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first integrated evidence linking consumer-defined behavioral phenotypes of food neophobia to objective biological markers. It will contribute to the field by offering a biopsychosocial model of food choice, moving consumer research from correlation toward mechanistic explanation. The findings can ultimately help tailor feeding advice and food product development to different neophobia profiles based on their potential biological underpinnings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined either behavioral clusters or isolated biological correlates of neophobia, this study uniquely integrates these approaches by testing if specific biomarker profiles validate and explain previously identified consumer segments, a novel application of physiological methods within a well-established consumer segmentation framework.

proposals.title: Unconscious Barriers to Novel Food Acceptance: An Implicit Association Test (IAT) Study of Parental Biases and Their Effect on Adolescent Food-Related Life Satisfaction
proposals.motivation: Consumer acceptance of novel foods (e.g., plant-based proteins, insect-based ingredients) is critical for sustainable food systems, yet explicit surveys often fail to capture deep-seated, unconscious resistance. Pre-2026 research, including Grunert's work on food quality and parental feeding practices, suggests attitudes are complex and influenced by factors beyond conscious reasoning. Understanding these implicit biases is essential for developing effective communication and intervention strategies to improve dietary transitions.
proposals.hypothesis: Parents hold significant negative implicit biases (e.g., associating novel foods with 'disgust' or 'risk') compared to traditional foods, and the strength of these implicit biases moderates the relationship between their explicit food quality perceptions and their adolescent children's reported food-related life satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will employ a standard computerized Implicit Association Test (IAT) using the Greenwald et al. (1998) D-score algorithm. The IAT will contrast categories 'Novel Foods' (e.g., images of lab-grown meat, mycoprotein) and 'Traditional Foods' with attribute categories 'Good' (e.g., pleasant words) and 'Bad' (e.g., unpleasant words). This will be administered alongside explicit measures of food quality perceptions and the Satisfaction with Food-Related Life (SWFoL) scale to a sample of dual-earner parents and their adolescent children in a European country, building on Grunert's methodological expertise in cross-cultural invariance testing.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first direct measurements of implicit attitudes towards novel foods in a family context, extending Grunert's work on explicit drivers (e.g., pleasure, status). It will empirically test for a potential 'spillover effect' of parental implicit bias on adolescent well-being, offering a more nuanced model of household food decision-making. The findings will inform targeted behavioral nudges and communication strategies for the food industry and public health.
proposals.novelty: While IATs are established in social psychology, their application to novel food attitudes in a real-world parental decision-making context is novel. Integrating implicit measures with established constructs like SWFoL and parental feeding practices creates a unique multi-method framework to uncover unconscious barriers not captured by traditional food-related life or quality surveys.

proposals.title: Nudging Novelty: A Field Experiment on the Effect of Choice Architecture on Novel Food Acceptance in Family Settings
proposals.motivation: Promoting acceptance of novel, sustainable foods (e.g., plant-based proteins, insect-based ingredients) is crucial for dietary transitions, yet consumer resistance remains high. Pre-2026 research in behavioral economics demonstrates the power of nudges, but their application to novel food choice within the complex social context of family decision-making, particularly involving parents and adolescents, is under-explored. This gap is significant as family meals are a primary arena for food socialization and habit formation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, theory-driven nudges (e.g., default options, descriptive social norms, salience framing) implemented in a real-world choice architecture will significantly increase the selection and reported satisfaction with novel food items among parents, with a spillover effect on adolescent acceptance within the same household.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled field experiment in collaboration with a European food retailer or canteen. Using a 2x2 factorial design, we will manipulate the choice architecture for a target novel food product (e.g., a plant-based ready-meal). Nudges will include setting the novel item as the default option in a menu and displaying dynamic social norm information (e.g., '80% of parents in your city tried this last week'). Purchase data will be tracked via loyalty cards. Subsequently, a linked online survey (using the Satisfaction With Life Scale - SWLS and adapted items from the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire - CFPQ) will measure post-consumption satisfaction, perceived quality, and spillover to home feeding practices among a subset of participating dual-earner parents.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide causal evidence on the efficacy of behavioral nudges for novel foods in a real-world retail setting, moving beyond lab studies. It will extend Grunert's work on food quality perception and family feeding practices by integrating behavioral economics, and explore the 'spillover' concept from social media engagement to within-household behavioral contagion, offering practical insights for retailers and policymakers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in testing nudges not just on individual consumers but within the parent-adolescent dyad system, investigating intra-household spillover, and combining objective purchase data with validated psychosocial scales (SWLS, CFPQ) to understand the mechanism linking choice architecture to family-level food acceptance.

proposals.title: A Network Psychometric Analysis of Food Neophobia, Subjective Well-Being, and Family Food-Related Routines in Dual-Earner Households
proposals.motivation: Understanding the complex interplay between psychological traits, family dynamics, and food choices is crucial for promoting healthy diets. Pre-2026 research often treats constructs like food neophobia and well-being as independent latent variables, potentially missing their dynamic, reciprocal relationships within the family system, a key context for food socialization. This gap limits the design of effective interventions targeting food-related quality of life.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that food neophobia, subjective well-being, and specific family food habits (e.g., structured mealtime routines, parental modeling) form a connected psychological network, where central nodes (e.g., anxiety around new foods) act as potential intervention targets that can influence the entire system of well-being and habitual behaviors.
proposals.method: We will employ Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) and Ising model network analysis using the `qgraph` and `bootnet` packages in R. Data will be collected via an online panel from dual-earner parents in two countries (e.g., Denmark and Chile), measuring food neophobia (FNS), subjective well-being (SWLS, SWFoL), and family food practices (adapted CFPQ). We will estimate regularized partial correlation networks, assess stability via bootstrap, identify central nodes (strength, closeness), and test for network invariance across cultural contexts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, systems-oriented map of how food-related fears, life satisfaction, and daily routines co-constitute family food environments. It will move beyond linear effect models to identify key leverage points within these networks, offering a refined theoretical foundation for family-based nutritional interventions. It also extends network psychometrics into the substantive domain of household food consumer behavior.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is established in clinical psychology, its application to model the interrelations between food neophobia, general well-being, and specific family habits from a consumer research perspective is novel. It integrates Grunert's expertise in food quality perception and family feeding practices with cutting-edge, pre-2026 psychometric methods to visualize and quantify these relationships as an interactive system.

proposals.title: Capturing the Moment: An Ecological Momentary Assessment of Contextual Triggers and Parental Influences on Adolescent Food Neophobia
proposals.motivation: Food neophobia, the reluctance to try novel foods, is a significant barrier to healthy and diverse diets, particularly during adolescence. While cross-sectional studies link it to parental feeding practices, the real-time, contextual triggers (e.g., social setting, mood, food presentation) in daily life remain poorly understood. This gap limits the development of effective, just-in-time interventions to encourage novel food acceptance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) state-level factors (e.g., momentary stress, peer presence) will significantly predict real-time food neophobic reactions, and (2) these momentary relationships will be moderated by trait-level parental feeding practices, particularly pressure to eat and modelling.
proposals.method: We will implement a 14-day Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) study via a smartphone app. A sample of 150 adolescent-parent dyads will be recruited. Adolescents will receive 5 random prompts daily assessing context (location, social company), affective state (PANAS-Short Form), and any recent encounter with a novel or disliked food, rated for neophobic reaction. Parents will complete baseline measures, including the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ). Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling (MLM) to separate within-person (state) and between-person (trait) effects, with cross-level interactions testing parental practice moderation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, process-oriented map of food neophobia triggers in naturalistic settings. It will advance theory by testing how stable family-level factors (parental practices) interact with dynamic situational cues. The findings will directly inform the design of context-aware behavioral nudges and family-based interventions tailored to specific high-risk moments.
proposals.novelty: While EMA is used in eating behavior research, its application to food neophobia is novel. The integration of real-time adolescent data with validated parental practice measures (CFPQ) to examine cross-level dynamics represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance beyond static, retrospective surveys.

proposals.title: Beyond Neophobia: A Mixed-Methods Investigation of the Psychological Profiles and Family Food Socialization Antecedents of Extreme Food Neophilia
proposals.motivation: Food neophobia research has predominantly focused on the negative consequences of food rejection, leaving the opposite extreme—extreme food neophilia (very low neophobia)—largely unexplored. Understanding this group is crucial for developing a complete model of food choice behavior and could inform strategies for promoting dietary variety and novel food acceptance in the general population. This study addresses a significant gap in consumer food psychology by shifting the focus from barriers to drivers of adventurous eating.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals with extreme food neophilia possess distinct psychological profiles (e.g., higher sensation-seeking, openness to experience) and report childhood feeding practices and family food environments characterized by higher parental encouragement, modeling of variety, and lower pressure, compared to a normative control group.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential mixed-methods design. First, an online survey will recruit a large sample (N>1000) using the Food Neophobia Scale (FNS) to identify extreme neophiles (lowest 10%) and a matched control group. The survey will measure psychological traits (BFI-2, sensation-seeking), childhood feeding practices (adapted from the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire), and current food-related life satisfaction (SWFoL). Quantitative analysis will use MANOVA and structural equation modeling to test group differences and relationships. Second, in-depth interviews (n=30-40) with selected extreme neophiles will qualitatively explore the lived experience and perceived familial antecedents of their food adventurousness.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, empirically grounded profile of the extreme food neophile, a neglected segment in food consumer research. It will extend models of food choice by explicitly modeling the positive extreme of the neophobia-neophilia continuum and offer novel insights into the family socialization practices that may foster dietary openness, complementing existing knowledge on practices that reduce neophobia.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Grunert and others has examined drivers of specific food choices (e.g., pork in China) and feeding practices, this proposal uniquely inverts the dominant neophobia paradigm to study its opposite. It innovatively combines established scales (FNS, CFPQ, SWFoL) with qualitative methods to explore the under-theorized positive extreme of food adventurousness and its developmental roots.

proposals.title: Trauma-Induced Food Neophobia in Adults: A Dual-Earner Parent Study on the Erosion of Familiar Food Acceptance
proposals.motivation: Food neophobia is well-studied in children, but its emergence in adults towards previously familiar and accepted foods, triggered by trauma or illness (e.g., severe food poisoning, COVID-19 anosmia, or cancer treatment), is poorly understood. This phenomenon has significant implications for individual nutritional status, family meal dynamics, and quality of life, particularly in dual-earner households where food-related stress is already high. Understanding its drivers is crucial for developing targeted interventions to support affected individuals and families.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the development of neophobia towards familiar foods in adults is a distinct psychological construct from general food neophobia, directly mediated by post-traumatic stress symptoms and negatively moderated by parental feeding practices focused on balance and structure (e.g., 'healthy eating guidance').
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-country, cross-sectional survey targeting dual-earner parents with adolescent children, leveraging Grunert's expertise in cross-cultural measurement. The survey will integrate adapted scales from the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ), the Food Neophobia Scale (FNS), and the PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5). We will employ Structural Equation Modelling (SEM) with latent variables to test the mediation and moderation effects. Data analysis will use the lavaan package in R, specifically testing for configural and metric measurement invariance across country samples (e.g., Chile, Denmark) to ensure construct validity.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first validated model of trauma-induced familiar food neophobia, distinguishing it from developmental neophobia. It will extend the application of the CFPQ to a novel context of adult psychopathology and inform the development of support strategies for healthcare professionals and family therapists dealing with food-related trauma sequelae.
proposals.novelty: While neophobia is typically framed as a childhood trait, this study uniquely conceptualizes it as an acquired adult condition triggered by specific adverse events. It innovatively links trauma psychology with food choice research and parental feeding practices, a nexus previously unexplored.

proposals.title: Bridging the Sustainability Intention-Action Gap: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Multi-Format Information Interventions on Parental Food Choice
proposals.motivation: Despite high general concern for sustainability, a persistent intention-action gap exists in consumer food choices, particularly for everyday products like meat. This gap is critical for families, where parents' purchasing decisions shape household environmental impact and model behaviors for children. Pre-2026 research, including Grunert's work on pork in China and feeding practices, identifies this disconnect but lacks tested interventions to effectively bridge it at the point of choice.
proposals.hypothesis: Information interventions that combine cognitive (e.g., detailed eco-labels) and affective (e.g., narrative messaging about future generations) elements will be more effective in translating general sustainability concern into specific sustainable product choice than interventions using either element alone, particularly among dual-earner parents who experience time pressure and decision fatigue.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 2x2 between-subjects online randomized controlled trial (RCT) with a simulated shopping task. Participants (N=800 dual-earner parents from a European panel) will be randomly assigned to one of four label/information conditions: 1) Control (standard label), 2) Cognitive (traffic-light eco-score), 3) Affective (short narrative on intergenerational impact), 4) Combined. The primary dependent variable is the choice of a sustainable pork product over a conventional one. We will measure general sustainability concern (SWFL* life satisfaction scale adapted for environmental well-being), perceived information sufficiency, and choice satisfaction. Analysis will use logistic regression with moderation analysis to test the interaction between intervention type and baseline concern.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide causal evidence on the efficacy of specific information formats to close the sustainability gap, directly contributing to behavioral food policy and sustainable marketing. It will extend the literature on customer engagement spillover by testing if engagement with sustainability information spills over into actual choice. Furthermore, it will offer practical insights for designing effective product labels and campaigns targeted at families.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents the intention-action gap, this study experimentally tests *how* to bridge it by integrating cognitive and affective communication theories within a food choice context for parents—a key yet understudied demographic. It moves beyond measuring attitudes to testing concrete interventions in a ecologically valid choice task.

proposals.title: The Global Food Label User: A Multi-Country Analysis of the Interaction Between Demographics, Human Values, and Information Use
proposals.motivation: Food label use is a key mechanism for informed consumer choice and public health, yet its drivers are complex and culturally contingent. Prior cross-country studies often treat demographics and values as independent predictors, missing their synergistic effects. Understanding these interactions is crucial for designing effective, targeted food information policies and marketing strategies in a globalized food market.
proposals.hypothesis: The influence of core human values (e.g., security, hedonism, universalism) on food label use is systematically moderated by demographic factors (e.g., parental status, income, age), and these interaction patterns vary significantly across national contexts with differing food cultures and regulatory environments.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, harmonized online survey in six strategically selected countries (e.g., Denmark, Germany, Chile, China, USA, Poland) representing diverse food cultures and economic development. The survey will measure food label use (dependent variable), demographics, and human values using the refined Schwartz Portrait Values Questionnaire (PVQ-RR). Data analysis will employ multi-group structural equation modeling (SEM) with latent moderated structural equations (LMS) to test for interaction effects within and between countries. Measurement invariance of the value and label-use constructs will be rigorously tested using the SWFOL (Satorra–Bentler scaled chi-square difference) method to ensure valid cross-cultural comparison.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a nuanced, interactionist model of global food label use, moving beyond main-effect explanations. It will offer empirical evidence on how value-demographic profiles shape information processing, directly informing the tailoring of consumer communication. The study will also contribute methodologically by demonstrating the application of advanced invariance testing and latent interaction models in cross-cultural food consumer research.
proposals.novelty: While Grunert's prior work has examined drivers like pleasure or status in single countries and established measurement invariance for constructs like feeding practices, this study uniquely integrates human values theory with demographic moderators in a multi-country design to explain label use, a previously underexplored interaction in a global context.

proposals.title: The Shock Effect: How a Major Food Safety Crisis Alters Consumer Valuation of Traceability Information in Meat Products
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, food traceability systems are often promoted for their quality and safety assurances, yet consumer willingness-to-pay (WTP) for traceability alone remains limited in many markets, as it is often conflated with other credence attributes like organic or premium quality. A major food safety crisis represents a critical exogenous shock that can disrupt established consumer beliefs and decision-making heuristics, providing a unique natural experiment to test the fundamental value of traceability information when safety concerns are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to a major, geographically proximate food safety crisis (e.g., a meat contamination scandal) will significantly increase consumer WTP for traceability information alone, independent of other product attributes, and that this effect will be moderated by individual factors such as parental status, food-related life satisfaction, and conscientiousness.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, between-subjects design combining a survey and discrete choice experiment (DCE). Pre- and post-crisis data will be collected from dual-earner parents in a relevant market (e.g., Chile or China). The DCE will isolate the traceability attribute using a fractional factorial design. We will analyze the data using hybrid choice models, integrating latent variables (e.g., food-related life satisfaction from SWFoL scales, conscientiousness) to model heterogeneity. The core analysis will involve testing for measurement invariance in key constructs pre- and post-crisis and estimating the causal effect of the crisis shock on the traceability attribute's part-worth utility.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide causal evidence on how exogenous shocks reshape consumer valuation of food information, moving beyond correlational studies. It will advance the application of hybrid choice models in food marketing by integrating crisis exposure as a structural variable. The findings will offer actionable insights for policymakers and industry on the resilience and value proposition of traceability systems post-crisis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines static drivers of traceability WTP, this study uniquely leverages a real-world safety crisis as a natural experiment to causally identify its impact. It innovatively combines Grunert's expertise in parental feeding practices, life satisfaction, and customer engagement spillover to test novel moderators of this shock effect.

proposals.title: Beyond the Average Consumer: A Latent Class Segmentation of Food Purchase Drivers with a Focus on Traceability and Origin
proposals.motivation: Food marketing and policy often treat consumers as a homogeneous group, leading to inefficient communication and product development. While traceability and origin are recognized as important quality cues, their relative importance compared to other drivers like price, taste, or convenience is poorly understood at a segment level. This research addresses a critical gap by identifying distinct consumer segments where these credence attributes are the primary purchase motivators, enabling more targeted strategies for sustainable and high-quality food systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that latent consumer segments exist where traceability and/or origin are the dominant drivers of food choice, and that these segments can be characterized by specific socio-demographic profiles, food-related lifestyles (FRL), and levels of subjective well-being and life satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional survey (n≈1200) in a European country, using established scales for food-related lifestyle (FRL), subjective well-being (SWB), and life satisfaction (SWLS). The survey will include a choice experiment or best-worst scaling to measure the relative importance of purchase drivers (traceability, origin, price, brand, etc.). Latent Class Analysis (LCA) will be the core analytical method to identify homogeneous consumer segments based on their driver importance profiles. Post-hoc, we will characterize the identified 'traceability/origin-centric' segment(s) using ANOVA and chi-square tests against other segments.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, data-driven typology of consumers based on primary purchase drivers, moving beyond generic importance ratings. It will offer actionable insights for producers and retailers on how to position traceability and origin information for maximum impact on specific target groups. Theoretically, it will integrate food choice models with well-being and lifestyle constructs to explain segment membership.
proposals.novelty: While segmentation studies exist, few combine a fine-grained analysis of purchase driver hierarchies (using advanced discrete choice methods) with a comprehensive psychological and lifestyle characterization to explain why specific segments emerge, particularly focusing on credence attributes like traceability.

proposals.title: Beyond Freshness: A Comparative Analysis of Label Effects on Consumer Evaluations of Fresh Pork and Processed Sausages
proposals.motivation: Consumer research on meat labeling has predominantly focused on fresh products, investigating drivers like quality, pleasure, and status. However, processed meat products (e.g., sausages) represent a significant market segment with distinct perceived risks and benefits, often linked to health and naturalness concerns pre-2026. Understanding whether established label effects (e.g., origin, quality seals, nutritional claims) transfer from fresh to processed contexts is crucial for effective food policy and marketing, especially given parental concerns about family diet quality.
proposals.hypothesis: The effect of informational labels (e.g., organic, nutritional content, origin) on perceived quality, healthiness, and purchase intention is moderated by product type, with labels having a weaker or differently valenced effect for processed sausages compared to fresh pork due to the overriding influence of perceived processing-related risks.
proposals.method: The study employs a 2 (Product Type: fresh pork chop vs. pork sausage) x N (Label Conditions) between-subjects experimental design, administered via an online survey. Building on Grunert's prior methods, we will measure key dependent variables: perceived product quality, healthiness, and purchase intention. The analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM) with multi-group analysis to test for measurement invariance and moderation, specifically examining the interaction between product type and label information on outcome constructs, controlling for consumer life satisfaction and conscientiousness as potential moderators of label processing.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first direct empirical test of how label effect mechanisms scale across the fresh-processed meat continuum. It will contribute to theory on food choice by integrating product categorization and risk perception into the study of information effects. Practically, it will offer evidence-based guidance for tailoring label communication strategies for different meat product categories.
proposals.novelty: While label effects on fresh meat are documented, their application to processed meats is not straightforward due to the 'health halo' of fresh products versus the 'negative processing heuristic.' This study's novelty lies in explicitly testing this boundary condition, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all understanding of meat labeling.

proposals.title: Quantifying Parental Trade-offs: A Conjoint Analysis of Meat Attributes in Family Food Choice
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 context, understanding the drivers of meat purchase is crucial for public health and sustainable food systems. Parents, as key household food gatekeepers, face complex trade-offs between price, sensory quality, and credence attributes (e.g., animal welfare, environmental impact) when making choices for their families. However, the relative importance and willingness-to-pay for these attributes in a family context, particularly considering parental feeding practices and life satisfaction, remain insufficiently quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for dual-earner parents, credence attributes related to animal welfare and sustainability will have a significant, positive effect on meat choice utility, but this effect will be moderated by parental feeding practices (e.g., restriction, monitoring) and mediated by perceived information sufficiency.
proposals.method: We will employ a choice-based conjoint (CBC) analysis, a well-established marketing method for quantifying trade-offs. A fractional factorial design will generate profiles varying price, eating quality (tenderness, juiciness), and credence attributes (production method, welfare certification, carbon footprint). Data will be collected via an online survey from a sample of dual-earner parents in a European country. We will use hierarchical Bayes (HB) estimation within a multinomial logit model to derive individual-level part-worth utilities and calculate relative importance scores and willingness-to-pay. The moderating role of feeding practices (measured via the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire) and the mediating role of information satisfaction will be tested using latent class analysis and structural equation modeling (SEM).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, quantitative map of parental valuation of meat attributes, directly extending Grunert's work on pork drivers in China to a European family context. It will contribute to the literature by integrating conjoint analysis with psychosocial constructs (feeding practices, life satisfaction) to explain heterogeneity in food choices, offering actionable insights for policymakers and marketers aiming to promote sustainable and healthy diets.
proposals.novelty: While conjoint analysis is established, its application to model how parental feeding practices and life satisfaction systematically moderate the valuation of meat attributes for family consumption is novel. This bridges the gap between discrete choice modeling in food marketing and the psychosocial determinants of family food-related life (SWFoL).

proposals.title: Forecasting Meat Consumption Trends: An Integrated Model of Attitudes, Social Norms, and Parental Influences
proposals.motivation: Global meat consumption trends are shifting due to health, environmental, and ethical concerns, creating significant uncertainty for food systems and policy. Existing forecasts often rely on economic or demographic variables, failing to capture the psychological and social drivers of food choice. A model integrating these factors is urgently needed for more accurate scenario planning in the pre-2026 context of rising alternative protein markets and evolving consumer values.
proposals.hypothesis: A comprehensive food choice model, integrating attitudes, subjective and descriptive social norms, and parental feeding practices, will significantly improve the accuracy of forecasting meat consumption trends compared to traditional socio-economic models.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-country longitudinal survey (e.g., in China, Chile, and a European nation) targeting dual-earner parents with adolescent children, leveraging Grunert's expertise. The survey will measure constructs from the Theory of Planned Behavior (attitudes, norms, perceived control), the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ), and life satisfaction. Using Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), we will test the integrated model's fit and predictive validity. For forecasting, we will employ a multi-group cross-lagged panel analysis within the SEM framework to model trends, and use the validated parameter estimates to run agent-based simulation scenarios of future consumption.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated, psychologically-grounded forecasting tool for meat consumption, directly applicable by industry and policymakers. It will advance theoretical understanding by explicitly modeling the transmission of food norms within families and their longitudinal effect on aggregate trends, bridging micro-level consumer behavior and macro-level market shifts.
proposals.novelty: While food choice models are well-established, their systematic application to forecast aggregate meat consumption trends is novel. The integration of parental feeding practices and family dynamics into a forecasting model for a commodity like meat represents a significant departure from existing economic or attitudinal models.

proposals.title: Beyond the Welfare Claim: A Discrete Choice Analysis of Competing Quality Cues in Consumer Food Choice
proposals.motivation: Consumer research and industry marketing often position animal welfare as a primary, high-involvement quality cue for meat products, particularly in Western markets. However, in real-world, budget-constrained shopping scenarios, cues like price, brand familiarity, and visual appearance may exert a stronger influence on actual choice, creating an attitude-behavior gap. Understanding the relative weight of these competing cues is crucial for accurately modeling consumer decision-making and for developing effective, honest food quality communication strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: In a discrete choice experiment simulating a realistic purchase context, extrinsic cues (price and brand) will have a significantly larger influence on the choice of fresh pork than the intrinsic cue of animal welfare certification, even among consumer segments that express high concern for welfare in surveys.
proposals.method: We will employ a state-of-the-art online discrete choice experiment (DCE) with a 2^3 full factorial design (welfare certification: yes/no; price: high/low; brand: national/private label). Data will be collected from a representative sample of primary grocery shoppers in Germany. To analyze the choice data, we will use a Hierarchical Bayes (HB) estimation procedure for a Mixed Logit model, which accounts for preference heterogeneity. The analysis will quantify the part-worth utilities and relative importance scores of each attribute, and latent class analysis will be used to identify segments based on choice patterns.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, choice-based evidence on the real trade-offs consumers make, moving beyond stated preferences. It will contribute methodologically to the field of food-related quality perception by integrating DCE with segmentation analysis. The findings will offer practical insights for producers and retailers on how to effectively position welfare-assured products in a competitive market landscape.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Grunert and others has identified welfare as a driver of *purchase intention*, this study directly tests its *competitive weight* against the powerful, habitual cues of price and brand in a controlled yet ecologically valid choice task, challenging the assumed primacy of welfare in the final decision moment.

proposals.title: Beyond the Interview: An Ethnographic Study of Real-Time Quality Decision-Making in Family Food Procurement and Preparation
proposals.motivation: Understanding food quality perceptions is central to consumer behavior and public health, yet reliance on self-reported data from surveys and interviews is a major limitation. Pre-2026 research, including Grunert's work on pork in China and feeding practices in Chile, highlights a gap between stated preferences and actual behavior, especially in the complex, time-pressured contexts of dual-earner parents. A deeper, context-rich understanding is needed to bridge this intention-action gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ethnographic observation in naturalistic shopping and cooking settings will reveal a set of tacit, situational, and socially negotiated quality decision-making heuristics among parents that are systematically different from the rational, attribute-based models derived from interview data alone.
proposals.method: The study employs a multi-sited ethnographic design. We will recruit 15-20 dual-earner families with adolescent children in a European urban setting. Researchers will conduct accompanied shopping trips and in-home cooking observations, using continuous narrative field notes, photo documentation (with consent), and brief contextual interviews. Data analysis will follow a rigorous qualitative protocol: 1) Thematic analysis of field notes to identify decision points and cues, 2) Systematic coding using NVivo software (pre-2026 version) to categorize behaviors, and 3) Triangulation with brief post-observation interviews to member-check interpretations and explore stated versus observed reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, contextually grounded framework of food quality decision-making processes, moving beyond static attribute lists. It will provide methodological advancement by demonstrating how ethnographic methods can be systematically integrated into consumer research to validate and enrich survey-based findings, offering more actionable insights for nutrition education and food marketing.
proposals.novelty: While ethnography is established in anthropology, its systematic application to test the validity of survey-based models of food quality perception in the natural contexts of busy family life is novel in consumer research, offering a direct empirical challenge to the assumption that stated drivers (e.g., pleasure, status) align with in-situ behavior.

proposals.title: From Qualitative Themes to National Patterns: A Large-Scale Survey on Parental Food-Related Life Satisfaction and Quality Perceptions
proposals.motivation: Prior qualitative work has identified complex themes linking parental food provisioning, perceived food quality, and overall life satisfaction, but these relationships lack quantitative, generalizable evidence. Understanding how these factors interact at a national level is crucial for developing effective public health policies and targeted consumer communication, especially in a pre-2026 context where family food practices are under pressure from economic and time constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a positive spillover effect exists, where parents' perceptions of food quality directly mediate the relationship between their food-related practices (e.g., planning, involvement) and their broader subjective well-being and life satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nationally representative survey (n=~3000) in a European country (e.g., Denmark), using stratified random sampling. The questionnaire will integrate validated scales, including the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ) adapted for broader food-related practices, the Satisfaction with Life Scale (SWLS), and a custom-built multi-dimensional food quality perception module. Data analysis will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) using Mplus or the lavaan package in R to test the proposed mediation model, controlling for socio-demographics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative map of the connections between parental food practices, quality perceptions, and life satisfaction, moving beyond niche qualitative insights. It will validate and extend the spillover effect concept from social media engagement to the core domain of family food consumption, offering empirical evidence for policymakers and the food industry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling intimate qualitative themes to a national survey and explicitly testing food quality perception as a key mediator in the parent well-being ecosystem, a specific pathway not yet quantitatively established in pre-2026 consumer research.

proposals.title: Neural Correlates of the Health-Taste Trade-off: An fMRI Study of Parental Decision-Making for Functional Foods
proposals.motivation: The success of functional foods depends on consumer acceptance, yet a persistent gap exists between stated health intentions and actual purchase behavior, particularly for parents making choices for their families. Traditional methods like surveys (e.g., the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire) capture explicit attitudes but fail to reveal the implicit, automatic brain processes underlying the trade-off between perceived health benefits and anticipated taste. Understanding this neural conflict is crucial for developing effective communication strategies and product formulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the evaluation of functional foods will elicit simultaneous activation in brain regions associated with reward (e.g., ventral striatum) and cognitive control (e.g., dorsolateral prefrontal cortex), and that the relative strength of these activations will predict stated purchase intention and mediate the effect of health information on choice.
proposals.method: We will employ functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) with a well-established food evaluation paradigm. A sample of dual-earner parents will view images of conventional and functional food products (e.g., regular yogurt vs. probiotic yogurt) while in the scanner. The design will include a 2 (health claim: present/absent) x 2 (product type) factorial structure. Neural responses will be analyzed using a general linear model (GLM) approach with SPM12 or FSL software. Region-of-interest (ROI) analyses will focus on a priori networks for taste reward, health valuation, and conflict resolution, and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analysis will assess functional connectivity during trade-off decisions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first neuroscientific evidence mapping the implicit decision conflict in functional food choices within a parental context. It will extend Grunert's work on drivers of food quality perception and feeding practices by identifying the neural mechanisms that may explain the 'intention-behavior gap.' The findings will offer a novel, biologically-grounded framework for segmenting consumers and tailoring health messages.
proposals.novelty: While prior consumer neuroscience has studied food, no pre-2026 work has specifically applied fMRI to the health-taste trade-off for functional foods, nor has it focused on the parent-child decision unit, integrating Grunert's expertise in family feeding practices with neuroimaging methods.

proposals.title: Regulatory Frameworks and Consumer Trade-offs: A Cross-National Analysis of How Food Policies Shape Perceived Quality, Safety, and Pleasure
proposals.motivation: Global food markets are governed by diverse national regulations (e.g., EU precautionary principle, US risk-based approach, China's evolving standards), which directly influence product formulation, labeling, and availability. Pre-2026 consumer research, including Grunert's work on pork in China, identifies key trade-offs (e.g., pleasure vs. safety, quality vs. price) but lacks a systematic analysis of how these perceived trade-offs are structurally shaped by the regulatory environment. Understanding this link is crucial for policymakers aiming to design effective regulations and for companies navigating international markets.
proposals.hypothesis: The stringency and communicative focus of a country's food regulatory framework systematically moderate the perceived trade-offs between key food choice drivers (e.g., sensory pleasure, healthiness, safety, convenience) among consumers, with stricter, more transparent regimes reducing perceived conflicts between safety/health and pleasure.
proposals.method: The study employs a multi-method, multi-country design. First, a content analysis will codify the regulatory frameworks of 6-8 nations (e.g., Germany, USA, Chile, China, Japan) on dimensions of stringency and communication clarity using pre-defined scoring algorithms. Second, an online survey, using established scales (e.g., SWFL, food-related lifestyle instruments) and customized items, will measure perceived trade-offs and driver importance among parents (n=800 per country). Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with Multi-Group Analysis (MGA) will test for measurement invariance and then model the regulatory dimensions as country-level moderators in a hierarchical model, analyzing their effect on the latent trade-off structures.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, empirically grounded model linking macro-level regulatory structures to micro-level consumer psychology. It will advance the field of food-related quality perception by moving beyond descriptive cross-cultural comparisons to a causal explanatory framework. The findings will offer actionable insights for harmonizing international food standards and improving risk communication strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares consumer perceptions across cultures, this study innovatively quantifies and integrates objective regulatory characteristics as explicit moderating variables into consumer choice models, moving from correlation to a test of regulatory influence on cognitive trade-off structures.

proposals.title: Inverting the Consumer Mindset: A Computational Design Framework for Functional Foods Minimizing Perceived Parental Trade-offs
proposals.motivation: The functional foods market is growing, but adoption is often hindered by consumer-perceived trade-offs between health, taste, convenience, and naturalness. Traditional product development analyzes these perceptions post-hoc, leading to costly reformulations. An inverse design approach, starting from a deep understanding of parental decision-making frameworks, is needed to create products that align with holistic consumer values from the outset, thereby increasing market success and public health impact.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting established structural equation models (SEM) of parental food-related life satisfaction and quality perceptions, it is possible to computationally derive optimal product attribute profiles that minimize perceived trade-offs for specific consumer segments.
proposals.method: Leveraging pre-2026 methods, we will first construct a robust SEM using survey data from dual-earner parents, linking functional food attributes (e.g., nutrient content, processing level) to latent constructs of perceived quality, health belief, and convenience, and ultimately to feeding practice satisfaction. We will then employ an inverse optimization algorithm (e.g., a gradient-based approach or genetic algorithm) on the fitted SEM. This algorithm will treat the model's structural coefficients as constraints and iteratively search for the input vector of product attributes that maximizes a composite output score of perceived value while minimizing the variance (trade-off) between key latent constructs.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, theory-driven computational tool for the food industry to design products that are intrinsically more acceptable to target segments like busy parents. It advances consumer science by operationalizing the 'inverse problem' paradigm, moving from analyzing drivers of choice to engineering choice drivers.
proposals.novelty: While SEM is standard for analyzing consumer perceptions, its systematic inversion for product design is novel in food research. This approach integrates Grunert's work on quality perception and parental feeding practices with computational optimization, a non-obvious synthesis pre-2026.

proposals.title: Beyond the Quality Imperative: Identifying and Profiling Consumer Segments with Dominant Price or Convenience Orientations in Food Choice
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 consumer food research, including Grunert's own work, often implicitly assumes quality perceptions are the primary driver of choice, treating price and convenience as secondary trade-offs. This assumption may obscure the existence of stable consumer segments for whom these attributes are the permanent, dominant choice heuristics. Understanding such segments is crucial for accurate market segmentation, effective public health nutrition policies, and sustainable business strategies, particularly in cost-of-living crises.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within food markets, there exist distinct, stable consumer segments for whom either price sensitivity or convenience-seeking permanently dominates perceived quality as the primary decision criterion, and that these segments can be profiled by distinct socio-demographic, psychological, and life-satisfaction characteristics.
proposals.method: The study will employ a cross-national survey (e.g., EU/China) using choice experiments with fresh pork and ready meals as stimulus products. We will apply Latent Class Analysis (LCA) to identify segments based on choice utilities for quality, price, and convenience attributes. Subsequently, we will use the SWFoL (Satisfaction With Food-related Life) scale, measures of subjective well-being (life satisfaction), and demographic variables (including dual-earner parent status) to profile the identified segments, testing for measurement invariance across countries. Moderation effects of conscientiousness (from prior social media spillover research) on segment membership will also be explored.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated typology of quality- vs. price- vs. convenience-dominant food consumers, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all quality model. It will contribute to theory by integrating food-related life satisfaction and general well-being into the profiling of hard-nosed utilitarian segments. Findings will offer actionable insights for targeted marketing and policy communication.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly challenging the foundational assumption that quality is the universal primary driver, instead theorizing price and convenience as stable, segment-defining master heuristics, and linking these segments to broader psychological constructs like SWFoL and life satisfaction previously associated with quality-seeking.

proposals.title: Measuring the Trust Premium: Applying Keller's Brand Equity Model to Sustainability and Health Credence Attributes in Meat Consumption
proposals.motivation: Consumers increasingly rely on credence attributes like 'organic', 'antibiotic-free', or 'carbon-neutral' when purchasing meat, but these claims are difficult to verify post-purchase, making trust paramount. However, the marketing field lacks robust, standardized frameworks to quantify the equity and value generated by trust in such claims, hindering effective communication and policy. This gap is critical for producers, retailers, and policymakers aiming to promote sustainable and healthy diets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Keller's Customer-Based Brand Equity (CBBE) pyramid model can be successfully adapted to measure the equity of credence attributes, and that the strength of this 'credence attribute equity' mediates the effect of claim exposure on purchase intention and willingness-to-pay for meat products.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-country online survey targeting meat-purchasing parents, leveraging Grunert's expertise in cross-cultural food studies. The survey will adapt Keller's CBBE dimensions (Salience, Performance, Imagery, Judgments, Feelings, Resonance) to specific credence attributes. Using Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with Mplus, we will test the proposed mediation model. The analysis will include measurement invariance testing (as in Grunert's prior work on the CFPQ) to ensure cross-cultural comparability and examine moderating effects of consumer conscientiousness and information-seeking behavior.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated, transferable framework for quantifying the intangible value of trust in food credence attributes, moving beyond simple claim recognition. It will offer actionable insights for marketers on building credible communication strategies and inform policymakers on which trust-building mechanisms (e.g., certifications, traceability) most effectively translate claims into consumer value and behavioral intention.
proposals.novelty: While brand equity models are well-established, their systematic application to measure the equity of specific product *attributes*, particularly intangible credence claims in the meat category, is novel. This bridges classic marketing theory with contemporary food consumer science focused on sustainability and health.

proposals.title: When Trust Fails: A Multi-Country Study of Consumer Skepticism Towards Extrinsic Food Quality Cues
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of quality labels and certifications (e.g., organic, fair trade) is a key market response to consumer demand for information, yet their effectiveness hinges on trust. Pre-2026 research, including Grunert's work on pork in China, shows cue usage varies widely, but systematic analysis of active distrust and rejection of such cues is lacking. Understanding this skepticism is crucial for policymakers and firms aiming to design credible quality signaling systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that consumer rejection of extrinsic quality cues is driven by a combination of perceived cue inauthenticity and low general institutional trust, and that this effect is moderated by consumer values (e.g., status vs. sustainability orientation) and product category involvement.
proposals.method: The study employs a multi-method design. First, a cross-sectional survey in three countries (e.g., Denmark, Chile, China) will measure rejection of specific cues, using established scales for institutional trust, consumer skepticism, and values (drawing on SWFL and life satisfaction constructs for broader well-being context). Second, a discrete choice experiment will quantify the 'rejection effect' by including a 'no-label' option and manipulated label attributes. Data analysis will use structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the hypothesized relationships and multi-group analysis to assess measurement invariance and cross-cultural differences.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated model of the antecedents of quality cue rejection, moving beyond studies of acceptance. It will offer actionable insights for segmenting consumers based on trust profiles and cue receptivity. The cross-cultural comparison will clarify how institutional contexts shape cue credibility, informing international marketing and regulation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines cue use, this proposal explicitly focuses on the understudied phenomenon of active distrust and rejection. It uniquely integrates the literature on institutional trust and consumer values within a food choice framework, testing for spillover effects of general skepticism onto specific product evaluations.

proposals.title: Crisis, Calories, and Compromise: A Multi-Country Panel Analysis of Food Quantity and Quality Substitution in Households with Children
proposals.motivation: Economic and public health crises, such as the COVID-19 pandemic and inflationary periods, place significant strain on household food security. Policymakers and food system actors operate on the common assumption that households first reduce food quality (e.g., switching to cheaper, less nutritious brands) before reducing the quantity of food consumed, but this hierarchy of coping strategies lacks robust, multi-context empirical validation. Testing this claim is crucial for designing effective social safety nets and nutritional interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: During a significant economic or public health crisis, household food consumption quantity (measured in calorie availability) remains stable, while food quality (measured by dietary diversity, product tier, and nutritional content) shows a significant decline. This effect is moderated by parental characteristics, including employment status and feeding practices.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal, multi-country panel design, leveraging pre-existing household scanner data (e.g., Kantar, Nielsen) and linked survey data from Chile and a European nation, spanning pre-crisis, crisis, and recovery periods. The method involves a cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) to test temporal precedence and causal direction between quantity and quality indicators. We will use structural equation modeling (SEM) with latent variables for 'food quality' (derived from product category spend shares and a Healthy Eating Index) and 'crisis impact' (a formative index of income shock and stress). Moderation analysis will test the effects of dual-earner status (from Grunert's prior work on the CFPQ) and life satisfaction.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first multi-context, longitudinal test of the quality-quantity substitution hypothesis in food consumption during crises, moving beyond anecdotal evidence. It will extend Grunert's work on parental feeding practices by examining how these practices interact with macroeconomic shocks. The findings will offer evidence-based guidance for targeting food aid and consumer communication.
proposals.novelty: While prior research documents crisis impacts on diet, this study uniquely disentangles the temporal dynamics and causal precedence of quantity versus quality adjustments using panel data and CLPM. It innovatively integrates macroeconomic shock indicators with micro-level consumer psychology constructs (parental feeding practices, life satisfaction) within a single model.

proposals.title: Crisis-Driven Choice Shifts: A Latent Class Analysis of Agri-Food Purchasing and Nutritional Quality During Economic and Supply Chain Disruptions
proposals.motivation: Global crises, such as the COVID-19 pandemic and geopolitical conflicts, have profoundly disrupted agri-food supply chains and household economies, forcing rapid adaptations in consumer behavior. Understanding how these crisis-driven shifts affect the nutritional quality of household food baskets is critical for public health and food policy, yet existing research often lacks a granular, model-based analysis of the heterogeneous consumer segments and choice mechanisms involved.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, crisis-responsive consumer segments (e.g., 'security-first', 'price-sensitive', 'habit-sticklers') emerge during disruptions, and that membership in these segments, moderated by household structure (e.g., presence of children), systematically predicts changes in the nutrient density of purchased food baskets.
proposals.method: We will employ advanced discrete choice modeling, specifically a Latent Class Choice Model (LCCM), to analyze household scanner panel data (e.g., from Kantar or Nielsen) spanning pre-crisis and crisis periods. The model will estimate class-specific utility functions for product attributes (price, brand, convenience, perceived shelf-life) and class membership probabilities based on household characteristics (income, parental status). The output will be linked to a nutrient database to calculate a Nutrient-Rich Food (NRF) index for each household's basket, allowing for difference-in-differences style analysis of nutritional change by latent class.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, segment-specific understanding of how crises alter the nutritional trajectory of food consumption, moving beyond aggregate averages. It will provide actionable insights for policymakers targeting nutritional support and for food marketers aiming for responsible portfolio adaptation during future disruptions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating latent class consumer choice theory from marketing with nutritional outcome analysis to model crisis-driven behavior, explicitly testing for the moderating role of parental status—a key theme in Grunert's work—on segment formation and nutritional impact.

proposals.title: Inferring Household Welfare Dynamics from Nutritional Quality Shifts: A Structural Equation Modeling Approach in Dual-Earner Families
proposals.motivation: Traditional household welfare metrics (income, expenditure) are often lagging, coarse, or unreliable. Shifts in the nutritional quality of household food purchases, however, provide a real-time, behavioral indicator of economic pressure or prosperity. This is particularly salient for dual-earner families with children, where time constraints and budget allocation decisions directly impact dietary quality, as suggested by Grunert's prior work on feeding practices and pork purchase drivers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal changes in the nutrient density (e.g., mean adequacy ratio for key micronutrients) and food category composition (e.g., processed vs. fresh) of household food baskets are causally linked to latent changes in subjective and objective household welfare, mediated by parental time pressure and moderated by household socioeconomic characteristics.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-group, longitudinal Structural Equation Model (SEM). The model will treat household welfare as a latent variable constructed from pre-2026 indicators: subjective life satisfaction, financial satisfaction (SWFOL), and debt-to-income ratio. Nutritional quality data (scanner or diary-based) will be processed to create indices for energy density, nutrient profile, and food processing level (NOVA classification). Cross-lagged panel models will test the directionality of effects between welfare and diet quality, while multi-group analysis (e.g., in Mplus) will assess measurement invariance and moderation effects across different parent and country contexts, building on Grunert's methodological expertise.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel methodological framework for using high-frequency food purchase data as a leading indicator of household economic well-being, useful for policymakers and researchers. It will deepen understanding of the bidirectional relationship between economic resources and dietary quality within family systems, extending Grunert's work on food quality drivers and parental feeding practices into the economic domain.
proposals.novelty: While prior research links socioeconomic status to diet quality, this study inverts the causal direction to *derive* welfare from dietary data. It uniquely integrates economic, psychological (satisfaction), and detailed nutritional metrics within a formal SEM framework to model dynamic, reciprocal relationships over time, a novel synthesis of Grunert's research streams.

proposals.title: Assessing Mode Equivalence in Food Consumer Research: A Multi-Trait Multi-Method Validation of Online vs. Offline Surveys for Measuring Meat Preferences and Quality Perceptions
proposals.motivation: Online surveys are a dominant, cost-effective tool in consumer food research, but their validity for capturing complex, sensory-driven constructs like meat preferences compared to traditional offline methods (e.g., paper-based, in-person) remains under-examined. Given Klaus Grunert's extensive work on food quality perception and the increasing reliance on digital data collection, especially post-2020, it is critical to empirically test for measurement invariance to ensure findings are not artifacts of the survey mode. This is vital for robust cross-cultural comparisons and longitudinal studies where modes may mix.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a well-designed online survey will demonstrate configural, metric, and scalar measurement invariance with an equivalent offline survey for key constructs related to meat preferences (e.g., perceived quality, purchase drivers, satisfaction). Any observed mean differences between samples will be attributable to demographic composition, not systematic mode bias.
proposals.method: We will employ a between-subjects, multi-trait multi-method (MTMM) design. A structured questionnaire, based on Grunert's prior scales (e.g., from pork purchase drivers study), will measure meat quality perceptions, consumption motives, and feeding practices. It will be administered identically in two modes: (1) an online panel (e.g., via Qualtrics) and (2) an offline, paper-based survey in controlled community settings. Data will be analyzed using confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) and multi-group CFA in Mplus or R's lavaan package to test for measurement invariance across modes. We will also use the SWFL (Satisfaction With Life) scale as a methodological benchmark to assess spillover effects of mode on broader well-being questions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide concrete, empirical evidence for the validity of online surveys in food consumer research, strengthening the methodological foundation of the field. It will offer best-practice guidelines for designing mode-equivalent surveys and directly inform the interpretation of Grunert's and others' cross-national research on food preferences. The findings will enhance the credibility of online data for industry and policy decisions related to meat consumption.
proposals.novelty: While mode effects are studied generically, a focused investigation on the specific, affect-laden, and culturally variable domain of meat preferences is novel. Integrating MTMM with invariance testing specifically for food-related constructs and parental feeding practices, and using life satisfaction as a control trait, provides a nuanced methodological contribution beyond simple comparisons of mean scores.

proposals.title: Drivers of Fresh Pork Purchase in China: A Nationally Representative Analysis of Quality, Pleasure, and Status Motivations
proposals.motivation: Understanding the drivers of meat consumption in rapidly developing markets like China is crucial for food policy, sustainability, and marketing. Pre-2026 research, including Grunert's pilot study, identified pleasure, quality, and status as key motives but was limited in generalizability. A broader, nationally representative sample is needed to validate these constructs across diverse demographic and regional segments, providing a robust evidence base for stakeholders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while pleasure, perceived quality, and status-seeking will all significantly influence purchase intentions for fresh pork in China, their relative importance and interaction effects will vary systematically across consumer segments defined by region, urbanization level, and socio-economic status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, cross-sectional online survey using a stratified random sampling design to recruit a nationally representative sample of 3,000 regular pork consumers in China. The survey instrument will replicate and extend the validated scales from the pilot study (e.g., quality perceptions, hedonic attitudes, status motivation). Data analysis will employ Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) to test the main driver model, followed by Multi-Group SEM (MGSEM) to test for measurement invariance and compare structural coefficients across key demographic and geographic segments, as informed by pre-2026 methods like those used in the Chilean feeding practices study.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first nationally representative map of psychosocial drivers of pork consumption in China, significantly enhancing the external validity of existing models. It will contribute to the field of food-related consumer behavior by empirically demonstrating how fundamental motives interact with macro-level segmentation variables, offering actionable insights for targeted public health and sustainable consumption strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the driver constructs themselves, but in rigorously testing their structural relationships and relative weights across a truly representative national population, moving beyond localized or convenience samples which dominate pre-2026 literature on food choice in emerging economies.

proposals.title: A Cross-Cultural Comparison of Food-Related Life Satisfaction and Parental Feeding Practices in European Families: Poland, Germany, and Denmark
proposals.motivation: Understanding how national contexts shape food-related attitudes and family practices is crucial for developing effective public health and marketing strategies in the integrated European market. Pre-2026 research, including Grunert's work on food-related life satisfaction (SWFoL) and parental feeding practices, has often focused on single countries or non-European comparisons, leaving a gap in systematic intra-European analysis. This study addresses this by comparing Poland, a post-transition economy with strong traditional values, with established Western and Northern European nations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant cross-national differences exist in the drivers of food-related life satisfaction and the use of specific parental feeding practices, moderated by cultural values and socio-economic factors. Furthermore, we hypothesize that the relationship between parental feeding practices and adolescent food well-being will vary in strength across these cultural contexts.
proposals.method: We will employ a quantitative survey design using established, validated scales: the Satisfaction with Food-Related Life (SWFoL) scale and the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ), adapted for cross-cultural use. Data will be collected from a matched sample of dual-earner families with adolescent children (aged 12-17) in Poland, Germany, and Denmark (N≈600 per country). Analysis will involve multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (MGCFA) to establish measurement invariance, followed by structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the hypothesized structural relationships and moderation effects by country. Advanced statistical packages like Mplus or the lavaan package in R will be used for these analyses.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a nuanced, comparative map of European food cultures at the family level, directly informing tailored nutritional education and family-focused food policy. It will extend Grunert's models of food-related quality of life and parental feeding practices by empirically testing their generalizability and boundary conditions across distinct European cultural settings.
proposals.novelty: While cross-cultural food studies exist, few simultaneously integrate the constructs of food-related subjective well-being and detailed parental feeding practices within a rigorous comparative framework of European families. The focus on the under-researched Polish context in direct comparison with Western European benchmarks provides a novel lens on post-transition consumer evolution.

proposals.title: From Farm to Fork: Causal Mechanisms Linking Pig Contact Frequency, Emotional Dissonance, and Pork Consumption Intentions
proposals.motivation: Understanding the psychological drivers of meat consumption is critical for promoting sustainable and ethical food systems. While a correlation between animal contact and consumption is suggested, the underlying causal mechanisms—particularly the role of emotional and cognitive dissonance—remain unexplored in pre-2026 literature. This gap limits the ability to design effective interventions for consumers, especially parents who make household food decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that frequent, positive contact with pigs increases emotional attachment, which in turn triggers cognitive dissonance when considering pork consumption, ultimately leading to reduced consumption intentions. This relationship will be moderated by individuals' pre-existing food-related values (e.g., quality, status) and their strategies for dissonance reduction.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-method study. First, a cross-sectional survey in a European country with both conventional and alternative pig farming will measure pig contact frequency, emotional attachment (using adapted Human-Animal Bond scales), cognitive dissonance, and pork consumption intentions. Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) will test the proposed mediation model. Second, a between-subjects experimental vignette study will manipulate contact frequency (high vs. low) and the salience of the animal's identity to establish causality, measuring subsequent intention to purchase pork products. Analysis will use ANOVA and moderated mediation models.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first causal model linking animal contact to meat consumption via psychological dissonance mechanisms, directly extending Grunert's work on food quality and purchase drivers. It will offer novel insights for the fields of consumer psychology, food ethics, and sustainable consumption by identifying key moderators that explain variance in consumer responses.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying and testing dissonance theory to the 'contact-consumption' paradox, moving beyond mere correlation, and integrating it with established food-related value constructs from Grunert's prior work on pork in China and parental feeding practices.
