proposals.title: From Field to Fork: A Longitudinal Case Study of Farmer Attitudes, Food Quality Perceptions, and Household Consumption Shifts
proposals.motivation: Understanding the drivers of sustainable food systems requires insight into the producers themselves. Pre-2026 research, including Grunert's work on pork in China, often relies on cross-sectional consumer surveys, creating a gap in understanding how farmers' own attitudes and quality perceptions evolve and influence their household consumption. This is critical for policies aiming to align agricultural production with nutritional and environmental goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant on-farm events (e.g., adopting a new practice, experiencing a market shock) trigger measurable changes in a farmer's subjective perception of food quality, which subsequently spill over to alter household food consumption patterns, mediated by their parental feeding practices.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month deep longitudinal case study with a purposively sampled cohort of 15-20 dual-earner farmer families. Using a mixed-methods approach, we will employ repeated semi-structured interviews, photo-elicitation, and a tailored survey battery administered quarterly. The survey will include validated scales: the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ) to assess parental practices, the Satisfaction with Life Scale (SWLS), and custom items on food-related lifestyle and perceived food quality. Qualitative data will be analyzed via thematic analysis, while quantitative trajectories will be modeled using Latent Growth Curve Modeling (LGCM) to test the effect of discrete events on attitude and consumption pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rare process-oriented view of the farmer-consumer nexus, detailing the mechanisms of attitude-behavior change within agricultural households. It will extend spillover theory, typically applied in marketing, to the agricultural domain and offer empirical evidence on the measurement invariance and contextual applicability of the CFPQ in a novel population (farmers).
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a consumer psychology and household dynamics lens to the producer, tracking intra-household spillover from professional farming experiences to private consumption, a perspective largely absent in pre-2026 agri-food studies.

proposals.title: The Producer-Consumer Perception Gap: A Comparative Analysis of Stated Beliefs and Actual Preferences in the Pork Supply Chain
proposals.motivation: Efficient food value chains depend on accurate market signals. Prior to 2026, research on consumer food choice (e.g., Grunert's work on pork in China) is extensive, but the upstream perception of these preferences by producers and food scientists is understudied. This gap can lead to misaligned production, marketing, and R&D strategies, reducing chain sustainability and consumer satisfaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pork producers and agricultural scientists systematically misperceive the relative importance of key consumer preference drivers (e.g., pleasure, quality, status, sustainability), and that this perception gap is moderated by the actors' own professional values and information sources.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-phase, multi-country survey. First, we measure actual consumer preferences using a discrete choice experiment (DCE) with attributes identified from pre-2026 literature (e.g., price, origin, ethical claims). Second, we survey producers and scientists, presenting them with the same DCE attribute set and asking them to predict the average consumer's choice utility weights. The perception gap will be quantified using paired t-tests and regression analysis. We will employ the SWFL (Satisfaction with Life) scale and measures of professional ethos to test for moderating effects, using structural equation modeling (SEM) with latent variables.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative mapping of the 'producer-consumer perception gap' in an agri-food context, extending Grunert's Means-End Chain theory upstream. It will identify specific belief biases and their drivers, offering actionable insights for improving communication and co-creation within the food chain, ultimately enhancing market orientation and reducing waste.
proposals.novelty: While consumer perception of products is well-researched, this study inverts the lens to systematically analyze producer perception *of the consumer*, a critical but neglected link. It innovatively applies consumer research methods (DCE, SEM) to an upstream actor sample to diagnose systemic misalignments.

proposals.title: Quantifying Parental Trade-offs: A Conjoint Analysis of Nutritional Labels and Sustainability Claims in Family Food Choices
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 food market, product labels are overloaded with competing information on nutrition, origin, and environmental impact. For parents, who are key gatekeepers of household food quality, this creates complex decision-making scenarios where trade-offs between health, price, and sustainability are poorly understood. Grounded in Grunert's work on food-related life and quality perception, this study addresses a critical gap in understanding how dual-earner parents navigate these conflicting signals to achieve satisfaction in family feeding practices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for dual-earner parents, the relative importance (part-worth utility) of nutritional claims (e.g., 'high in protein') will significantly outweigh that of abstract sustainability claims (e.g., 'carbon neutral') in a direct trade-off, but that this effect will be moderated by the parent's level of consumer life satisfaction and conscientiousness.
proposals.method: We will employ a choice-based conjoint (CBC) analysis, implemented via Sawtooth Software's Lighthouse Studio or a comparable platform. Parents will be presented with a series of choice tasks between product profiles defined by attributes and levels (e.g., label: nutritional claim vs. sustainability claim vs. origin claim; price: three levels; certification: present/absent). Hierarchical Bayes (HB) estimation will be used to calculate individual-level part-worth utilities and relative importance scores. These will be analyzed alongside survey measures of comprehensive feeding practices, consumer life satisfaction (SWFOL), and conscientiousness to test for moderation.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a quantitative, utility-based map of the trade-offs parents make between label attributes, moving beyond stated preferences. It will contribute to the literature on food-related life by linking specific label utilities to broader consumer dispositions and life satisfaction. The findings will offer actionable insights for policymakers and food marketers aiming to effectively communicate product qualities to family decision-makers.
proposals.novelty: While conjoint analysis is established in marketing, its application to dissect the specific trade-offs of dual-earner parents between nutritional and sustainability labels is novel. Integrating the resulting utilities with psychological constructs like conscientiousness and life satisfaction, as suggested by Grunert's recent spillover effect research, creates a unique socio-cognitive model of food label evaluation.

proposals.title: From Label to Life: A Microsimulation Model of the Long-Term Public Health Impact of Front-of-Pack Nutrition Labels in European Populations
proposals.motivation: Front-of-pack (FOP) labeling is a key policy tool to combat diet-related non-communicable diseases. However, evidence on its effectiveness is largely limited to short-term changes in consumer understanding and purchase intentions. A critical gap exists in quantifying the long-term, population-level health outcomes of different FOP schemes (e.g., Nutri-Score, Multiple Traffic Lights, Warning Labels) to inform evidence-based policy decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interpretive, summary indicator FOP labels (like Nutri-Score) will lead to greater aggregate improvements in dietary quality at the population level compared to reductive, nutrient-specific labels, resulting in a more significant reduction in the burden of cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes over a 10-year period.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-time microsimulation model using pre-2026 data (e.g., EU national food consumption surveys, food composition databases, and disease risk equations from cohort studies). The model will simulate individual life courses, incorporating a decision module where food choices are influenced by FOP labels based on pre-2026 estimates of label efficacy from meta-analyses. Health outcomes will be projected using established risk algorithms (e.g., for CVD). We will employ probabilistic sensitivity analysis to account for uncertainty in input parameters.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first comparative, quantified projection of the long-term public health impact of major European FOP labeling schemes. It will offer policymakers a concrete, model-based tool for anticipating the health gains of different regulatory options, moving beyond attitudinal measures to hard health outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by Grunert and others has measured the immediate effect of labels on consumer perceptions, this study innovatively scales these effects to long-term population health using microsimulation, directly linking food marketing research to public health forecasting.

proposals.title: Beyond the Price Barrier: A Multi-Method Analysis of Heterogeneity in Consumer Willingness-to-Pay for Food Quality and Health Attributes
proposals.motivation: Prevailing industry and policy narratives often assume a generalized consumer reluctance to pay for higher quality or healthier food, potentially stifling innovation and public health initiatives. However, pre-2026 consumer research, including Grunert's own work, indicates significant heterogeneity in food-related motivations (e.g., pleasure, status, health). A nuanced understanding of which consumer segments, under which conditions, *are* willing to pay a premium is critically needed for effective product development and communication.
proposals.hypothesis: Consumer willingness-to-pay (WTP) for food quality and health attributes is not uniformly low but is highly segmented and contingent on the interaction of product type (hedonic vs. utilitarian), perceived personal relevance, and individual difference factors such as food-related lifestyle (FRL) and conscientiousness.
proposals.method: The study employs a sequential mixed-methods design. First, a large-scale online survey in two contrasting markets (e.g., Germany and Chile) will measure WTP using choice-based conjoint analysis for product pairs varying in quality/health claims, price, and brand. This will be integrated with validated scales for Food-Related Life Satisfaction (SWFoL), the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ, adapted for personal consumption), and customer conscientiousness. Second, latent class analysis and structural equation modeling with moderation tests will identify distinct WTP segments and test for the spillover effects of general consumer engagement and conscientiousness on WTP, as suggested by Grunert's recent social media research.
proposals.contribution: This research will move the field from a blanket assumption of low WTP to a segmented, conditional model, identifying key leverage points for marketers and policymakers. It will empirically bridge Grunert's work on food-related lifestyle, family feeding practices, and engagement spillover into the core domain of economic valuation, offering a more holistic view of consumer food choice.
proposals.novelty: While WTP for quality is well-studied, this proposal uniquely integrates Grunert's specific constructs—food-related life satisfaction, parental feeding practice frameworks applied to adult self-regulation, and the conscientiousness moderation effect from social media engagement—into a unified WTP model, testing for novel cross-domain psychological spillovers.

proposals.title: The Dynamics of Food Quality Perception: A Longitudinal Analysis of Media Exposure, Food Scandals, and Parental Food Choices
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research often treats consumer quality perception as a static driver of food choice, despite evidence that media narratives and food safety scandals cause significant market disruptions. This is particularly critical for parents, whose food choices for their families are high-stakes and may be uniquely sensitive to evolving information. A dynamic understanding is needed to inform effective risk communication and brand management in an era of rapid information spread.
proposals.hypothesis: Parental perceptions of food quality for key product categories are not static but exhibit significant temporal volatility, which is systematically shaped by exposure to media coverage and the occurrence of food-related scandals, with this effect moderated by parental life satisfaction and information-seeking behavior.
proposals.method: We propose a three-wave longitudinal panel survey of dual-earner parents in a relevant market (e.g., Germany or Denmark), measuring perceived quality for selected staples (e.g., pork, dairy) alongside media consumption and trust metrics. The data will be analyzed using Structural Equation Modelling (SEM) with latent growth curves to model perception trajectories. We will employ a cross-lagged panel design to test temporal precedence and use multi-group analysis to test moderation by pre-measured life satisfaction (SWFoL) and conscientiousness, linking to Grunert's recent methodological work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal model of food quality perception dynamics, explicitly integrating media and scandal shocks. It will extend the spillover logic of customer engagement to the domain of risk perception. The findings will offer actionable insights for public authorities on crisis communication and for the food industry on managing brand equity over time.
proposals.novelty: It directly challenges the implicit stability assumption in quality perception models by applying a dynamic, longitudinal framework. It uniquely integrates constructs from Grunert's recent work—parental feeding practices, life satisfaction, and spillover effects—into a model of perceptual change driven by external information shocks.

proposals.title: The Moderating Role of Time Pressure: A Hybrid Choice Model of Price, Perceived Quality, Health, and Convenience Trade-offs in Food Choice
proposals.motivation: Contemporary food choices are complex, involving trade-offs between price, perceived quality, healthiness, and convenience. This is particularly salient for dual-earner parents, who face significant time constraints. Understanding how time pressure systematically alters these trade-offs is crucial for developing effective public health policies and consumer-centric marketing strategies in the pre-2026 food environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that time pressure acts as a key moderator, systematically increasing the relative importance of convenience and price while decreasing the weight given to perceived healthiness and intrinsic quality cues in food choice decisions.
proposals.method: We will employ a Hybrid Choice Model (HCM) integrating a structural equation model (SEM) for latent constructs (perceived quality, health perception) with a discrete choice experiment (DCE). The DCE will present food profiles varying on price, objective quality indicators, health claims, and convenience (preparation time). Time pressure will be manipulated between subjects (high vs. low). Model estimation will use Hierarchical Bayes (HB) methods via Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) simulation to capture individual-level heterogeneity in trade-offs under different conditions.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a quantified, causal model of how time pressure reshapes fundamental food choice heuristics. It will extend the Theory of Planned Behavior by formally integrating a situational constraint (time) into a utility-based framework. The findings will offer actionable insights for nutrition communication targeting time-poor families and for product development in the convenience food sector.
proposals.novelty: While trade-offs are well-studied, this proposal uniquely integrates the moderating effect of experimentally manipulated time pressure into a formal HCM, moving beyond correlational studies of time scarcity. It also bridges Grunert's expertise in quality perception and feeding practices with advanced behavioral modeling methods.

proposals.title: Beyond the Headline: A Longitudinal Panel Study of Parental Food Quality Perceptions, Trust Restoration, and Choice Dynamics Following a Major Food Scare
proposals.motivation: Food scares, such as the 2008 Chinese milk scandal or the 2011 German E. coli outbreak, cause profound but poorly understood long-term shifts in consumer behavior. Pre-2026 research, including Grunert's work on pork in China, often relies on cross-sectional data, failing to capture the evolution of quality perceptions and the complex process of trust restoration within households, particularly among parents responsible for family food choices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the impact of a food scare on perceived quality and choice is not uniform but follows distinct, segmented recovery trajectories among parents, moderated by their information-seeking behavior and general life satisfaction, with spillover effects to related product categories.
proposals.method: We propose a three-year longitudinal panel study of dual-earner parents in a European country recently affected by a significant animal protein scandal (e.g., poultry, pork). Data will be collected via a tailored smartphone app at quarterly intervals (T0-T11). The method integrates Grunert's recent approaches: (1) Using the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ) with established measurement invariance testing to track changes in parental feeding practices. (2) Applying structural equation modeling (SEM) with latent growth curve analysis to model perception trajectories. (3) Implementing a moderated mediation analysis to test if information exposure (via app-logged news consumption) and baseline life satisfaction (SWFoL scale) moderate the effect of perceived quality on choice recovery, while also testing for spillover to related categories.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed map of the temporal dynamics of consumer trust recovery in food, segmenting parents by their recovery pathways. It will empirically test the spillover of engagement/aversion to related products over time. The findings will offer evidence-based strategies for regulators and the food industry on targeted communication and restoration initiatives.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a longitudinal panel design with established psychosocial scales (CFPQ, SWFoL) and digital behavioral tracking to model dynamic, moderated recovery processes, moving beyond static snapshots of scare impacts.

proposals.title: The Stressful Bite: How Extreme Time Pressure Distorts Food Quality Perception and Choice in Single-Meal Decisions
proposals.motivation: In modern food environments, consumers increasingly make meal decisions under acute time pressure, a key stressor known to impair decision-making. Pre-2026 research on food choice often examines general patterns or aggregated purchases, neglecting the micro-level dynamics of a single, high-stakes meal decision. Understanding how stress alters the trade-offs between hedonic (pleasure), utilitarian (quality), and social (status) drivers identified in Grunert's prior work is crucial for developing realistic models of consumer behavior and for public health interventions targeting impulsive, low-quality food choices.
proposals.hypothesis: Under extreme time pressure, the perception of food quality for a single meal becomes dominated by a single, salient driver (e.g., immediate pleasure or convenience), suppressing the multi-attribute evaluation (quality, status, health) that occurs in deliberative choice. This leads to a systematic shift towards choices with higher perceived immediate gratification but lower objective nutritional quality.
proposals.method: We will employ a 2x2 within-subjects experimental design, manipulating time pressure (high vs. low) and meal context. Participants will make repeated single-meal choices from a validated set of options characterized on Grunert's key dimensions (pleasure, quality, status). The core analysis will use Structural Equation Modelling (SEM) to test for measurement invariance of the quality perception construct across stress conditions and to model the shifting path coefficients between latent drivers and final choice. Process data (response time, mouse-tracking) will be analyzed to capture decision process dynamics under stress.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a fine-grained, process-oriented model of how acute stress impacts the fundamental building block of food consumption: the single meal choice. It will extend the Theory of Planned Behavior by integrating a physiological stress moderator directly into the attitude-intention link for a concrete behavioral outcome. The findings will offer practical insights for the design of choice architectures (e.g., in canteens or food delivery apps) to nudge healthier decisions even under time constraints.
proposals.novelty: While the effect of stress on food choice is known, this proposal uniquely scales down the analysis to the single-meal level and explicitly tests for the collapse of multi-attribute evaluation under pressure, moving beyond simple correlations to model the causal cognitive process. It innovatively applies measurement invariance testing—a method used in Grunert's cross-cultural work—to the within-person, within-context domain of stress-induced decision-making.

proposals.title: The Producer's Mind: A Structural Equation Model of How Food Industry Actors Anticipate and Model Consumer Quality Perceptions
proposals.motivation: In food marketing, the accuracy of producers' and retailers' mental models of consumer perception directly impacts product success, resource allocation, and market efficiency. While Klaus Grunert's prior work extensively maps consumer drivers (e.g., pleasure, status), the reverse perspective—how supply-side actors perceive and predict these drivers—remains a critical blind spot, leading to potential mismatches between product offerings and consumer expectations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that food producers and retailers employ simplified, heuristic-based mental models of consumer quality perception, which systematically overemphasize tangible quality cues (e.g., certification) and underemphasize experiential and contextual drivers (e.g., pleasure, conscientiousness spillover effects) identified in contemporary consumer research.
proposals.method: We will conduct in-depth, semi-structured interviews with product managers and marketing executives in the pork and fresh produce sectors in two markets (e.g., Denmark and Chile). Using thematic analysis, we will codify their stated models of consumer decision-making. Subsequently, we will develop and test a structural equation model (SEM) using Mplus software, where latent constructs representing producers' perceived consumer drivers (independent variables) are linked to their strategic choices (dependent variables, e.g., marketing spend allocation). Measurement invariance testing (CFA) will assess model stability across cultural contexts, extending Grunert's recent methodological work.
proposals.contribution: This study will contribute a novel 'dual-process' framework to food marketing theory, explicitly linking supply-side cognition to market outcomes. It will provide practitioners with a diagnostic tool to identify gaps in their consumer understanding, thereby improving strategic alignment and potentially enhancing consumer satisfaction and life quality through better-targeted products.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the traditional consumer research paradigm to focus on the *producer's perception of the consumer*, a reflexive 'dual problem' rarely addressed empirically. It uniquely integrates Grunert's expertise in consumer drivers, cross-cultural measurement invariance, and spillover effects into a supply-side analysis.

proposals.title: Beyond Convenience: How Label Information and Parental Role Stress Shape Perceived Quality and Satisfaction with Ready Meals
proposals.motivation: Ready meals represent a critical, high-impact food category where consumer decisions balance convenience, health, and family well-being. Pre-2026 research shows widespread use of front-of-pack labels (FOPL) but persistent gaps in consumer understanding, particularly among time-pressed parents. This study addresses the urgent need to understand how label information is processed in a real-world, high-stakes context where purchase drivers extend beyond individual pleasure to include family feeding practices and parental satisfaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effect of nutritional label information (e.g., Nutri-Score, traffic lights) on perceived product quality and post-consumption satisfaction is moderated by parental role stress and mediated by the perceived alignment of the product with family feeding goals.
proposals.method: The study employs a mixed-methods, sequential explanatory design. First, a cross-sectional survey of dual-earner parents in a European market (e.g., Germany or Denmark) will measure label use, comprehension (using validated tasks), perceived product quality, life satisfaction, and parental role stress using the SWFoL (Satisfaction With Family Life) scale and related constructs. Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) will test the hypothesized moderated mediation. Second, follow-up laddering interviews with a sub-sample will explore the 'why' behind the statistical relationships, focusing on the spillover between product evaluation and broader family life satisfaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a nuanced, context-rich model of food label efficacy, moving beyond general populations to a key decision-making group. It will contribute to theory by integrating food-related quality perceptions with family psychology and subjective well-being. Practically, it will offer evidence-based guidance for policymakers and manufacturers on designing labels and communications that effectively support informed choices for families.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling down to a single, complex category to unpack the interaction between information processing and psychosocial context, and in applying Grunert's established quality perception framework to the understudied realm of ready meals through the lens of parental roles and spillover effects on family life.

proposals.title: The Dynamics of Food Label Use: A Pan-European Longitudinal Cohort Study on Policy, Life Stage, and Consumer Information Processing
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of front-of-pack nutrition labels (FOPLs) like Nutri-Score and Keyhole across Europe represents a major public health policy initiative. However, pre-2026 evidence on their long-term effectiveness is limited to cross-sectional or short-term studies, failing to capture how sustained exposure, life transitions (e.g., becoming a parent), and evolving policies dynamically shape consumer comprehension, use, and the resulting impact on dietary quality and life satisfaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal trajectories of food label use and information processing are non-linear, significantly influenced by critical life events (e.g., parenthood) and major policy changes (e.g., mandatory labeling implementation), and that these trajectories mediate long-term changes in perceived food quality and dietary satisfaction.
proposals.method: We propose establishing a multi-country, probability-based online panel (n=10,000) across five EU nations with varying FOPL policies. Data will be collected in four waves over three years using validated scales for label use (adapted from Grunert's food-related lifestyle), information processing, SWFoL (Satisfaction With Food-related Life), and the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire for parents. We will employ latent growth curve modeling (LGCM) to map individual trajectories, and time-lagged, multi-group panel analysis to test for causal effects of life events and policy shifts, controlling for demographic and conscientiousness moderators.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first pan-European evidence on the causal, long-term impact of labeling policies and aging on consumer behavior. It will extend theories of food-related life satisfaction by modeling label use as a dynamic mediator and offer policymakers a robust, longitudinal framework to evaluate the real-world efficacy of food information interventions across diverse populations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Grunert and others examines drivers of label use or feeding practices statically, this study's novelty lies in its longitudinal, multi-country cohort design that explicitly models the interaction between macro-level policy evolution and micro-level life course changes, capturing the spillover effects from policy to personal information processing to long-term well-being.

proposals.title: Bridging the Intention-Action Gap: A Multi-Method Field Study on the Contextual Moderators of Sustainable Food Label Use
proposals.motivation: The persistent attitude-behavior gap in sustainable consumption undermines the effectiveness of eco-labels and policy interventions. While self-reported label use is a common metric in surveys, its validity as a proxy for actual purchasing is questionable, leading to inaccurate market insights and ineffective strategies. This research directly addresses this methodological and substantive problem by identifying the precise conditions under which stated intentions fail to predict behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: The predictive validity of self-reported sustainable label use for actual purchasing is systematically moderated by a combination of contextual factors (e.g., time pressure, social presence) and individual traits (e.g., habit strength, conscientiousness), such that the gap is widest under high cognitive load and low trait conscientiousness.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal field study combining RFID-based in-store tracking of participants' product interactions with receipt scanning for purchase validation. Dynamic smartphone surveys (Ecological Momentary Assessment) will capture real-time intentions and contextual perceptions. Data analysis will employ a two-stage approach: first, using Random Forest algorithms to identify key moderators from the high-dimensional sensor and survey data; second, applying multilevel hierarchical linear modeling to quantify the isolated and interactive effects of these moderators (product category, time pressure, social context, habit strength) on the intention-behavior relationship.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a diagnostic, predictive model that specifies when and why self-reports are reliable, transforming a known methodological weakness into a tool for refining survey design and segmenting consumers. It will advance theory on the moderators of the intention-behavior gap by integrating real-world contextual data, and provide actionable insights for policymakers and marketers aiming to bridge this gap.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time, multi-method triangulation of intention, context, and behavior in a naturalistic setting, moving beyond lab studies and post-hoc surveys. The application of machine learning for moderator discovery within this rich behavioral dataset offers a non-obvious, data-driven approach to a classic problem in consumer research.

proposals.title: The Intention-Behavior Gap in Food Label Use: A Multi-Method Validation Study Comparing Self-Reports with In-Store Observations
proposals.motivation: Understanding consumer use of food labels (e.g., nutrition, origin, quality) is critical for public health and food policy, yet research predominantly relies on self-reported questionnaire data. Pre-2026 literature acknowledges a potential intention-behavior gap, but direct, rigorous validation of self-reported label use against actual in-store behavior is scarce. This limits the validity of models predicting food choice and the effectiveness of interventions based on such data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that self-reported frequency and importance of using specific food labels, as measured by established questionnaires, will show a statistically significant and practically meaningful divergence from observed label inspection behavior in a real shopping environment.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-method, within-subjects design. A sample of primary food shoppers (N=200) will first complete an online survey including the SWFL (Satisfaction with Food-Related Life) scale and a validated label-use questionnaire. Subsequently, their in-store behavior will be observed and recorded via discreet wearable eye-tracking glasses during a planned shopping trip. The core analysis will involve calculating congruence indices (e.g., Cohen's Kappa) between self-reported 'high use' of specific label types and observed visual fixation duration/count on those labels. We will use Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with latent variables to test for measurement invariance and model the effect of potential moderators (e.g., conscientiousness, satisfaction) on the self-report/observation discrepancy.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a robust, methodologically novel validation of a key measurement tool in food consumer research. It will quantify the intention-behavior gap for label use, offering concrete correction factors for future survey-based studies. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective consumer information campaigns and food labeling policies by highlighting where perceptions and actions misalign.
proposals.novelty: While the gap is theorized, this study's novelty lies in its direct, technologically-enabled (eye-tracking) behavioral validation within a real-world shopping context, combined with the application of advanced psychometric models (SEM for measurement invariance) to formally test the equivalence of the two measurement methods.

proposals.title: Beyond the Label: A Longitudinal EMA Study of Real-Time Food Label Engagement, Dietary Quality, and Metabolic Biomarkers in Dual-Earner Parents
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread policy focus on food labeling to improve public health, evidence for its direct impact on long-term dietary quality and metabolic health is mixed and largely based on retrospective self-reports. This gap is critical, as the effectiveness of labels depends on dynamic, in-the-moment engagement, which is poorly understood. A method capable of capturing this real-time behavior in natural settings is needed to link specific patterns of label use to objective health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, temporally-defined patterns of food label engagement (e.g., frequent, context-aware, and comprehension-driven use) captured via Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) will be longitudinally predictive of improved dietary quality scores and favorable changes in metabolic biomarkers (e.g., HbA1c, lipid profiles), particularly among time-pressured dual-earner parents.
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of dual-earner parents with adolescent children. Participants will complete a 6-month longitudinal study using a custom smartphone app for EMA. The app will trigger brief surveys at random and event-contingent moments (e.g., post-purchase) to capture label use frequency, context, and subjective comprehension. Dietary intake will be assessed via repeated 24-hour recalls. Fasting blood samples for metabolic biomarkers (HbA1c, lipids) will be collected at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months. Data analysis will employ multilevel growth curve modeling and time-lagged regression to examine if EMA-derived label engagement patterns predict subsequent changes in diet and biomarkers, controlling for baseline characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, ecologically valid evidence linking *processes* of label engagement (not just exposure) to objective health outcomes. It will advance the theoretical understanding of how information translates into behavior within the family food environment. The findings will offer concrete insights for designing more effective, targeted labeling interventions and communication strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the adaptation of EMA from environmental psychology to capture the micro-processes of food label use in real-time, combined with serial biomarker measurement. This moves beyond static surveys and laboratory studies to establish a dynamic, within-person causal chain from information exposure to physiological change.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Parental Trade-off: A Discrete Choice Experiment on Nutritional Labels, Price, and Brand in Family Food Purchases
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, public health policies increasingly mandate front-of-pack nutritional labelling (FOPL) to combat diet-related diseases. However, the effectiveness of such labels depends on how parents, as primary food gatekeepers, integrate this information amidst competing product attributes like price and brand trust. Understanding the specific trade-offs parents make is crucial for designing labels that genuinely shift consumer choice towards healthier options within real-world budget constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while nutritional label information (e.g., Nutri-Score, warning labels) significantly influences parental choice, its marginal utility is substantially lower than that of price for low-income families and lower than trusted brand equity for high-income families, creating a socioeconomic gradient in label effectiveness.
proposals.method: We will employ a state-of-the-art Discrete Choice Experiment (DCE) using a Bayesian D-efficient design. Parents of adolescent children (n=800, stratified by income) will complete an online survey with repeated choice tasks between product profiles varying in label type (5 levels), price (4 levels), brand (3 levels), and a 'no-choice' option. Data will be analyzed using Mixed Logit (MXL) models with correlated random parameters in R (Apollo package) or Stata, allowing for heterogeneous preferences. Willingness-to-pay for different label formats will be calculated from the estimated utility coefficients.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a precise, quantitative measure of the relative importance of label information against key commercial drivers in a family context. It will directly inform policymakers on the potential market-share impact of specific FOPL systems and offer marketers insights into how health cues compete with traditional marketing levers. It extends Grunert's work on food quality perception and parental feeding practices into a rigorous economic evaluation framework.
proposals.novelty: While DCEs are established, their application to dissect the parent-specific choice calculus between mandated labels and other attributes is novel. It uniquely integrates the 'parent' and 'quality' themes from Grunert's work with economic choice modeling, moving beyond attitudinal surveys to model actual trade-off behavior under policy-relevant conditions.

proposals.title: Beyond Demographics: A Latent Profile Analysis of Food-Related Lifestyle and Parental Feeding Practices in Highly Educated Female Consumers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 consumer segmentation in food research often relies on broad demographic categories like 'female' or 'better educated', assuming homogeneity within these groups. This masks significant behavioral diversity, limiting the effectiveness of targeted interventions for public health (e.g., obesity prevention) and marketing strategies. A more nuanced understanding of intra-group heterogeneity is needed to explain divergent behaviors in food quality perception, purchasing, and family feeding practices.
proposals.hypothesis: Within the demographic segment of highly educated female parents, distinct latent sub-segments exist, characterized by unique configurations of food-related lifestyle (FRL) and feeding practices, which significantly predict divergent outcomes in food choice quality, household food waste, and family life satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cross-sectional online survey in two contrasting European countries (e.g., Denmark and Spain) targeting dual-earner households with adolescent children. The survey will integrate established scales: the Food-Related Life Satisfaction (SWFoL) instrument, the Comprehensive Feeding Practices Questionnaire (CFPQ), and measures of food quality perception and conscientiousness. Latent Profile Analysis (LPA), a person-centered mixture modeling technique, will be applied to the FRL and CFPQ data to identify unobserved sub-groups. Subsequent MANCOVA and regression analyses will test for profile differences in outcome variables, controlling for covariates.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a refined, psychographically grounded typology of educated female consumers, moving beyond simplistic demographic proxies. It will directly inform the development of more precise public health messaging and food marketing strategies. Furthermore, it will extend the spillover effect framework by examining how consumer engagement profiles spill over into specific parental feeding domains and overall life satisfaction.
proposals.novelty: While segmentation studies are common, applying LPA to de-homogenize a key demographic group in the context of the food-parenting interface is novel. The study uniquely combines Grunert's SWFoL and CFPQ frameworks with a person-centered analytic approach to reveal latent, behaviorally meaningful segments that demographic variables alone cannot capture.

proposals.title: Can they trust the label? Testing the effect of educational interventions on consumer use of ISO and HACCP as quality cues in food choice
proposals.motivation: International quality and safety standards like ISO and HACCP are crucial for global food trade and consumer protection, yet their complexity often renders them opaque to end consumers. In the pre-2026 context of rising food safety concerns and demand for transparency, it is critical to understand if and how consumer education can transform these technical standards into effective quality signals that influence decision-making, bridging the gap between systemic assurance and individual perception.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted educational interventions that simplify and contextualize information on ISO/HACCP standards will significantly increase consumers' awareness, perceived diagnosticity, and subsequent use of these cues as indicators of food quality and safety in their purchasing decisions.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-method, longitudinal design. First, a survey (n=800) will establish baseline awareness and perceived value of ISO/HACCP among parents in two countries (e.g., Denmark and Chile), testing for measurement invariance. Second, a between-subjects online experiment will test three intervention types (informational video, interactive infographic, control) on their effect on cue use and purchase intention for pork products, analyzing data via structural equation modeling (SEM) with moderation (e.g., by conscientiousness). Third, a follow-up survey after two weeks will assess knowledge retention and spillover effects to related product categories.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of specific communication strategies for technical food standards, directly informing industry and policy outreach. It will extend models of food-related consumer decision-making by integrating formal quality certifications as a distinct cue type and testing its interaction with consumer traits and intervention formats.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines general quality cues (e.g., origin, brand), this study uniquely focuses on educating consumers about complex, system-level certifications (ISO/HACCP) and tests for longitudinal spillover effects, moving beyond one-off choice experiments to model sustained behavioral change.

The analogous problem—understanding how real-time environmental exposures affect behavior and physiology—is solved by using smartphone-based, in-the-moment data collection in natural settings. ideas.description: This research systematically identifies and quantifies the specific factors that moderate the relationship between self-reported label use and actual in-store purchasing behavior. It employs a longitudinal field study combining RFID-based in-store tracking, receipt scanning, and dynamic smartphone surveys to capture real-time intentions and contextual influences. The analysis uses machine learning and multilevel modeling to isolate the effects of product category, time pressure, social context, and individual traits like habit strength on the predictive validity of questionnaires. The outcome is a predictive model that specifies when self-reports are reliable proxies for behavior and when they are not, transforming a known problem into a diagnostic tool.

ideas.title: Bridging the Intention-Behavior Gap: A Calibrated Choice Modeling Framework for Food Labels
ideas.description: This research employs a sequential, multi-method framework to accurately quantify consumer trade-offs regarding label information. It begins with a traditional discrete choice experiment to capture stated preferences in a controlled, hypothetical setting. Crucially, it then incorporates a revealed-preference field component where participants make real purchasing decisions with monetary incentives, using their own funds or provided vouchers. By directly comparing stated and actual choices, the model can be statistically calibrated to correct for hypothetical bias and social desirability effects. This results in robust, behaviorally-valid estimates of the marginal utility and willingness-to-pay for label attributes that are predictive of actual market behavior.

ideas.title: Employ Adaptive Choice Modeling from Transportation Planning
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from transportation planning, where researchers model complex trade-offs in traveler decisions using adaptive stated choice experiments. The analogous solution uses efficient, often Bayesian, experimental designs that dynamically adjust attribute levels presented to respondents to improve the precision of utility estimates for hard-to-quantify attributes. Adapted to consumer research, this involves implementing an adaptive discrete choice experiment for food products. The survey would use an initial set of priors (from a pilot study or literature) and then, in real-time, adjust the combinations of label information (e.g., sustainability certifications, health claims), price, and brand presented to each consumer. This method would more efficiently and accurately quantify the marginal willingness-to-pay for specific label elements and the trade-offs consumers make, reducing respondent fatigue and improving the statistical efficiency of the choice model.

ideas.title: Validated Preference Measurement via Conjoint Analysis and In-Store Behavioral Field Experiment
ideas.description: This research employs a sequential mixed-methods design to overcome the hypothetical bias of traditional conjoint analysis. It first quantifies stated trade-offs between label attributes using an adaptive choice-based conjoint survey. The key innovation is the subsequent in-store field experiment, where a subset of products are equipped with digital labels that dynamically display the attributes studied in the conjoint task. By comparing purchases of treated versus control products and correlating this behavioral data with the stated preferences, the system empirically validates and recalibrates the utility weights, yielding a robust, behaviorally-grounded measure of consumer trade-offs.

ideas.title: The 'Knowledge-Action Gap' in Sustainable Shopping: A Behavioral Nudge Intervention
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that improving understanding of labels (e.g., environmental, ethical) will automatically increase their usage in shopping decisions. Drawing an analogy from Public Health and Behavioral Science, where a 'knowledge-action gap' is well-documented (e.g., people know smoking is bad but continue), the analogous solution involved implementing behavioral nudges in the choice environment rather than relying solely on education. The adapted research idea proposes an experiment to test if combining enhanced label understanding with subtle changes to the digital or physical shopping interface—such as default settings, simplified comparative displays, or salient visual highlights—can more effectively bridge the gap between consumer comprehension and the actual application of that knowledge in making a purchase, thereby isolating the effect of choice architecture from pure understanding.

ideas.title: Modeling Labeling Scheme Impact via Ecological Niche Competition
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from Community Ecology to model the aggregate public health impact of front-of-pack labeling schemes. In ecology, competition models (like Lotka-Volterra equations) are used to forecast how the introduction or dominance of one species changes resource use and community health. Analogously, we treat labeling schemes as competing informational 'species' within the food market ecosystem. The adapted idea simulates their competition for consumer choice, modeling how each scheme's 'market penetration' and effectiveness alters the population-level consumption of different food product categories. This systems-dynamics approach scales up from individual label effects to forecast aggregate dietary shifts, product reformulation responses, and ultimate public health outcomes, comparing the long-term population health impact of different labeling policy interventions.

ideas.title: Mapping the Ecology of Label-Use Habits: A Real-Time Heuristic Detection Framework
ideas.description: This research program uses Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) via smartphones to test the hypothesis that label use is primarily a habit or heuristic, not a deliberate choice. By prompting shoppers immediately before, during, and after a purchase, it captures the context (e.g., time pressure, cognitive load), the automaticity of label glancing, and the minimal information actually processed. The synergy creates a method to distinguish heuristic from deliberative label use by analyzing patterns across hundreds of real-world decisions, revealing how environmental cues and task characteristics trigger label-use habits.

ideas.title: Ecologically Valid, Multi-Method Framework for Assessing Cognitive Load's Impact on Label Comprehension and Use
ideas.description: This research moves beyond artificial lab tasks to investigate how real-world cognitive load influences label understanding. It employs experience-sampling and in-situ experiments within natural decision environments (like stores or websites) to measure both chronic user load and the effect of ecologically valid distractions. The framework triangulates data from behavioral choices, validated self-report scales, and objective physiological measures to create a robust model of the load-comprehension relationship. This approach directly tests how labels perform under the actual cognitive conditions they are designed for, yielding actionable insights for label design and policy.

ideas.title: Causal Decomposition of Label Impact via Integrated A/B Testing and Mediation Analysis
ideas.description: This research integrates large-scale A/B testing on an online grocery platform with a formal causal inference framework to isolate the true effect of label formats. The system uses stratified experimental design to control for known confounders and employs causal mediation analysis to quantify how much of the observed effect is directly attributable to the label versus mediated through changes in product ranking or price perception. This allows for the creation of a 'causal score' for each label format, providing actionable insights that are robust to the noisy, multivariable environment of a live e-commerce platform.

ideas.title: Dynamic Preference Architecture for Adaptive Label Formats
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond seeking a static compromise to actively model and design for the dynamic nature of consumer preferences between simplicity and comprehensiveness. It combines controlled experiments on choice architecture with psychometric modeling to deconstruct the perceived constructs of 'ease of use' and 'being informed.' The core innovation is a simulation environment where candidate label designs are evaluated not by aggregate preference scores, but by their stability and performance across simulated real-world contexts and user states. The outcome is a principled framework for generating adaptive label systems that can vary in information density or presentation based on detectable context, alongside clear guidelines for static labels that are most robust to preference dynamics.

ideas.title: Mapping the Mode-Switching Triggers of Label Use: From Deliberate Strategy to Habitual Heuristic
ideas.description: This research program rejects the simple dichotomy of the original idea and instead investigates the dynamic interplay between deliberate and habitual label use. It employs a combination of controlled lab experiments (varying time pressure, task complexity, and stakes) and ecological momentary assessment in real-world settings to track when and why individuals switch cognitive modes. The core hypothesis is that label use is a metacognitively guided strategy, where individuals rationally default to a habitual heuristic under predictable conditions (e.g., low stakes, high cognitive load) but deliberately engage with label information when the context demands it. The outcome is a predictive model of the situational triggers that govern this mode-switching behavior.

ideas.title: Causal: Isolating the regulatory signal in product reformulation using quasi-experimental design
ideas.description: This research isolates the causal effect of labeling regulations on product reformulation by implementing a quasi-experimental, longitudinal study. It constructs a panel dataset of detailed product formulations from manufacturers operating in both 'treatment' regions that enact a new label (e.g., Chile's warning labels) and matched 'control' regions that do not, tracking changes before and after the policy. Using a difference-in-differences econometric model, the analysis quantifies the share of reformulation attributable specifically to the regulation, net of broader industry trends. This design robustly answers whether labels directly drive healthier formulations or if observed changes are merely correlated with other factors.

ideas.title: Designing Decisive Labels: Reconciling Preference-Usage Gaps for Informed Choice
ideas.description: This research program investigates the design of product labels that reconcile the consumer tension between simplicity and informativeness while explicitly testing the hypothesis that such reconciliation is necessary but insufficient for driving usage. It posits that a novel class of 'decisive label formats' must be developed—formats that are cognitively efficient, satisfy conflicting preferences, and contain actionable decision triggers. The core empirical work will involve controlled experiments and field studies to identify which design elements (e.g., visual cues, information hierarchy, choice architecture integrations) successfully convert high comprehension into concrete selection or avoidance behaviors at the point of purchase, moving beyond mere comprehension or preference metrics to measure actual choice outcomes.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Passively Personalized Food Labeling via User-Controlled Data Pods
ideas.description: This system delivers personalized nutritional information without requiring manual user setup for each shopping trip. A user maintains a secure, portable data pod with their health conditions, dietary goals, and allergies. When a product is scanned, the system compares its complete compositional data against the pod's rules. The resulting label dynamically emphasizes critical, person-specific information while de-emphasizing less relevant data, all on a standard label format. This approach minimizes decision fatigue by providing passive personalization, ensuring relevance while preserving simplicity and user privacy through individual data control.

ideas.title: Causal Triangulation of Label Usage and Understanding via Longitudinal, Instrumental, and Mediator Designs
ideas.description: This research program uses a multi-method experimental and quasi-experimental framework to rigorously isolate causal mechanisms. A longitudinal panel tracks individuals over time with staggered introductions of labeling schemes, controlling for stable unobserved traits. Concurrently, instrumental variable approaches leverage arbitrary assignment variations as natural experiments. Finally, specific experiments manipulate not just the label's presence but also the hypothesized mediators of understanding, directly testing the causal pathway. This triangulation robustly addresses confounding and establishes whether label usage causes understanding, or if a latent common cause drives both.

ideas.title: Passive Sensing-Triggered Ecological Assessment for In-Store Behavior
ideas.description: This redesigned system uses smartphone and smartwatch sensors (GPS, Bluetooth beacons, accelerometer, heart rate) to passively detect when a participant enters a store, approaches a product shelf, and makes a selection. This sensor fusion creates a low-burden, event-triggered protocol that administers a single, one-question micro-survey immediately after a product interaction is sensed, capturing the decision rationale at the precise moment with minimal disruption. The continuous sensor data stream provides objective behavioral traces (e.g., dwell time, movement patterns) to contextualize and supplement the self-reported labels, creating a multi-modal, ecologically valid dataset of the shopping decision process.

ideas.title: Dual: Study manufacturer response to labels via evolutionary game theory
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from evolutionary biology to study manufacturer response to food labeling regulations. In ecology, evolutionary game theory models how populations of organisms adapt their strategies (e.g., prey defense mechanisms) in response to environmental pressures (e.g., predator behavior). Analogously, we can model food manufacturers as a population of agents adapting their product reformulation strategies in response to the 'selection pressure' of labeling regulations. The adapted study would use this formal framework to predict and classify common reformulation pathways (e.g., ingredient substitution, portion-size adjustment, nutrient dilution), identify stable industry-wide equilibria under different regulatory regimes, and ultimately provide a predictive model for how future label changes might trigger specific, and potentially unintended, manufacturer adaptations.

ideas.title: Diagnose context-specific barriers and codesign targeted interventions to increase label usage
ideas.description: This research begins with a diagnostic phase to move beyond generic assumptions and uncover the specific, contextual mechanisms—such as cognitive overload, mistrust, or misaligned incentives—that cause high understanding but low usage of labels. Using this causal model, researchers then collaborate with end-users to codesign and iteratively test highly targeted interventions, such as integrating labels into decision workflows or pairing them with trusted social proof. The final output is a validated, context-sensitive intervention package and a transferable methodology for diagnosing and bridging similar intention-action gaps in other domains.

ideas.title: Causal Segmentation Analysis of Label Formats Using Controlled Platform Experiments
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simple A/B testing by implementing a multi-platform, factorial experiment designed to isolate the causal impact of label formats. It uses a holdback control group on the primary platform and a matched control platform to difference out external trends. The design stratifies users by key segments (e.g., new vs. returning) and employs Bayesian hierarchical modeling to accurately estimate effects within and across these groups. This approach rigorously identifies not just if a label format works, but for whom and why, preventing the misleading conclusions of aggregate analysis.

ideas.title: Apply Clinical Trial Blinding Protocols to E-commerce Label Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the rigorous blinding methodology from clinical trials to the problem of testing label formats on e-commerce platforms. In clinical pharmacology, double-blind or triple-blind protocols are standard to eliminate participant and researcher bias when testing a new drug against a placebo. The adapted research idea applies this by designing a triple-blind A/B/C test for product labels. Shoppers see different label formats without knowing they are in a test, the data scientists analyzing purchase metrics are given anonymized variant codes, and the business team requesting the test is not informed which variant is which until the experimental period concludes and the data is unblinded. This method ensures the measured purchase impact is attributable solely to the label format's efficacy, not to internal biases or expectations, yielding more scientifically valid results for optimization decisions.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Heuristic vs. Deliberative Pathways in Label Processing Using Cognitive Load
ideas.description: This research program integrates cognitive load manipulations from psychology into a causal experimental design to actively test hypothesized pathways from regression models. By imposing high or low cognitive load during tasks involving labels (e.g., nutritional, warning, certification), the experiments can selectively inhibit the deliberate, analytical processing required for deep understanding while leaving heuristic, cue-based processing for superficial usage relatively intact. This allows for the causal disentanglement of these two pathways, determining whether high cognitive load causally decouples usage from understanding and identifying which label design elements are robust to or fail under constrained processing.

ideas.title: Adaptive Information Layering for Product Labels
ideas.description: This research seed adapts a solution from Human-Computer Interaction—specifically, the 'focus+context' and 'overview+detail' visualization techniques—to resolve the consumer conflict between desiring simple, easy-to-use labels and wanting to be fully informed. The analogous HCI solution allows users to interact with a simplified interface while having seamless access to detailed data layers. The adapted idea is to design studies that develop and test adaptive, layered label formats. These formats would feature a primary, streamlined view for quick comprehension and integrated, intuitive triggers (physical or digital) to access a full disclosure of information, thereby empirically investigating how such designs can satisfy both conflicting consumer preferences.

ideas.title: Apply Clinical Trial Adaptive Design to Label Format Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from adaptive clinical trials in pharmaceutical research to the problem of testing label formats in online grocery platforms. In clinical research, adaptive trial designs use interim analyses to modify ongoing studies, such as re-allocating participants to more effective treatments. Adapted to this context, the research would involve running a large-scale, multi-armed A/B/n test on a simulated or real grocery platform, but with a pre-planned adaptive design. The system would perform interim analyses on user engagement or purchase data and, based on pre-defined rules, automatically shift a larger proportion of incoming traffic to the label formats performing best, while potentially dropping poorly performing variants early. This methodologically rigorous adaptation increases the efficiency and ethical resource use of the testing process, accelerating the identification of the most effective labeling for health or sustainability outcomes.

ideas.title: Peripheral Vision Labels: Designing for Pre-Attentive Processing
ideas.description: This research idea inverts the need for sustained consumer attention by adapting a solution from Human-Computer Interaction and Information Visualization. The analogous problem is designing visual interfaces for rapid, low-cognition information acquisition, which is solved by leveraging pre-attentive visual attributes—properties like color, orientation, and size that are processed automatically by the human visual system before conscious attention. The adapted idea is to design 'Peripheral Vision Labels' for consumer products. By encoding critical information (e.g., 'organic,' 'high protein,' 'contains nuts') using robust pre-attentive visual channels, these labels would allow consumers to make accurate assessments or identifications under conditions of low attention, distraction, or extreme time pressure, effectively communicating before conscious focus is achieved.
